{{Infobox Egyptian deity |IDEOGRAM = G9 |NAME-EXTENDED = Written form |NAME = ra:Z1-G7-Axt:t*y |NAME-EXPLANATION = Ra-Horakhty
''Rˁ(w)-ḥr(.w)-3ḫ.tj''
''[[Ra (Egyptian mythology)|Ra]]-[[Horus]], Dweller of the Horizon'' |IMAGE1 = Stele Ra-Horakhty Louvre E5789.jpg |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 180px |IMAGE1-DESCRIPTION = Priest Renpetmaa with [[perfumed cone]] before Ra-Horakhty (Stele in the [[Louvre]], ca. 900 BCE, 22nd Dynasty) }} '''Ra-Horakhty''' (also spelled '''Re-Horakhty''' or '''Re-Harachte''') is a [[solar deity]] in [[Ancient Egyptian mythology]]. He emerged from the merging ([[syncretism]]) of the gods [[Ra (Egyptian mythology)|Ra]] and [[Horus of the Horizon|Horakhty]]. ## Representation Ra-Horakhty is typically depicted as a standing or seated man with red skin and a falcon head. On his head, he wears the [[sun disk]], encircled by the [[uraeus serpent]]. Other depictions show him as a man with a ram's head and sun disk, such as in the [[Mortuary Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|Temple of Seti I in Abydos]]. Additionally, he can appear with a lion's head, cat's head, or falcon head adorned with ram's horns or a sun disk, as seen in [[Hibis]]. ## Significance Ra-Horakhty is described as Ra, the solar god who rises in the east. This is the specific name of the god Ra as worshipped in [[Heliopolis (Ancient Egypt)|Heliopolis]].Rolf Felde: ''Egyptian Deities.'' Wiesbaden 1995, p. 51. In his role as the solar god of Heliopolis, he embodies the attribute of a "world ruler." Ra-Horakhty of [[Heliopolis]] was considered equivalent to the god [[Horus]] in [[Edfu]].M. Lurker: ''Dictionary of Gods and Symbols of Ancient Egypt.'' Bern et al., 1998, p. 91. [[Pharaoh]] [[Akhenaten]] referred to himself as the "High Priest of Ra-Horakhty." During the early years of his reign, he had the god depicted in his traditional form. In the first "[[Aten#The Didactic Name of Aten|didactic name]]" (Year 4–8) of the god [[Aten]], Akhenaten described him as Ra-Horakhty: “Long live the Horus of the horizon (Ra-Horakhty), who rejoices in the lightland (on the horizon).” ## Cult and Cult Centers The syncretic form of the two gods first appeared around 2445 BCE (5th Dynasty) in the [[Sun Temple of Niuserre]] at [[Abusir]] ([[Abu Ghurab]]). He was also worshipped in temples at Wadi es-Sebua, [[Amada]], [[Temple of Derr|Derr]], and the [[Great Temple of Abu Simbel]], constructed by [[Ramses II]]. Amada relief of Thutmose III.jpg|[[Thutmose III]] before Ra-Horakhty in the Temple of Amada - 001 Torino Museo Egizio geroglifici su pietra.jpg|Cartouches of the god Aten (First didactic name of Aten), mentioning Ra-Horakhty in the first [[Cartouche (Egyptian hieroglyph)|cartouche]] (read from right to left) Großer Tempel (Abu Simbel) 18.jpg|Great Temple of Abu Simbel Flickr - archer10 (Dennis) - Egypt-10C-024.jpg|Great Temple of Abu Simbel, sanctuary with Ra-Horakhty, Ramses II, [[Amun-Ra]], and [[Ptah]] (right to left) Maler der Grabkammer der Nefertari 001.jpg|[[Amentet]] in the form of [[Hathor]] and Ra-Horakhty (Tomb of [[Nefertari (19th Dynasty)|Nefertari]], [[QV66]]) ## Role in the Neo-Religious Movement Thelema In [[Thelema]], a neo-religious movement founded by [[Aleister Crowley]], Ra-Horakhty is venerated under the name ''Ra-Hoor-Khuit''. He represents one of the three core principles of Thelema's teachings. ''Ra-Hoor-Khuit'' is the speaker of the third chapter of the [[The Book of the Law]], the central holy text of the movement.Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. ## See Also * [[List of Egyptian deities]] ## Literature * [[Manfred Lurker]]: ''Dictionary of Gods and Symbols of Ancient Egypt.'' Special edition, Scherz, Bern et al., 1998, ISBN 3-502-16430-4, p. 91. * Rolf Felde: ''Egyptian Deities.'' 2nd expanded and improved edition, R. Felde Eigenverlag, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * [[Hans Bonnet (Egyptologist)|Hans Bonnet]]: ''Lexicon of Egyptian Religious History.'' Nikol, Hamburg, 2000, ISBN 3-937872-08-6, pp. 269, 629–630. ## External Links {{Commonscat|Ra-Horakhty|Re-Harachte}} ## References [[Category:Egyptian deities]] [[Category:Solar deities]] [[Category:Male deities]] [[Category:Animal deities]] [[Category:Hawk deities]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:new pages]][[en:draft:Re-Harachte]] [[Category:new pages]][[de:Re-Harachte]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:Https://ar.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:new pages]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[it:Ra-Horakhti]] [[es:Ra-Horajty]] [[ar:%D8%B1%D8%B9-%D8%AD%D9%88%D8%B1%D8%A7%D8%AE%D8%AA%D9%8A]]

''Rˁ(w)-ḥr(.w)-3ḫ.tj''
''[[Ra (Egyptian mythology)|Ra]]-[[Horus]], Dweller of the Horizon'' |IMAGE1 = Stele Ra-Horakhty Louvre E5789.jpg |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 180px |IMAGE1-DESCRIPTION = Priest Renpetmaa with [[perfumed cone]] before Ra-Horakhty (Stele in the [[Louvre]], ca. 900 BCE, 22nd Dynasty) }} '''Ra-Horakhty''' (also spelled '''Re-Horakhty''' or '''Re-Harachte''') is a [[solar deity]] in [[Ancient Egyptian mythology]]. He emerged from the merging ([[syncretism]]) of the gods [[Ra (Egyptian mythology)|Ra]] and [[Horus of the Horizon|Horakhty]]. ## Representation Ra-Horakhty is typically depicted as a standing or seated man with red skin and a falcon head. On his head, he wears the [[sun disk]], encircled by the [[uraeus serpent]]. Other depictions show him as a man with a ram's head and sun disk, such as in the [[Mortuary Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|Temple of Seti I in Abydos]]. Additionally, he can appear with a lion's head, cat's head, or falcon head adorned with ram's horns or a sun disk, as seen in [[Hibis]]. ## Significance Ra-Horakhty is described as Ra, the solar god who rises in the east. This is the specific name of the god Ra as worshipped in [[Heliopolis (Ancient Egypt)|Heliopolis]].Rolf Felde: ''Egyptian Deities.'' Wiesbaden 1995, p. 51. In his role as the solar god of Heliopolis, he embodies the attribute of a "world ruler." Ra-Horakhty of [[Heliopolis]] was considered equivalent to the god [[Horus]] in [[Edfu]].M. Lurker: ''Dictionary of Gods and Symbols of Ancient Egypt.'' Bern et al., 1998, p. 91. [[Pharaoh]] [[Akhenaten]] referred to himself as the "High Priest of Ra-Horakhty." During the early years of his reign, he had the god depicted in his traditional form. In the first "[[Aten#The Didactic Name of Aten|didactic name]]" (Year 4–8) of the god [[Aten]], Akhenaten described him as Ra-Horakhty: “Long live the Horus of the horizon (Ra-Horakhty), who rejoices in the lightland (on the horizon).” ## Cult and Cult Centers The syncretic form of the two gods first appeared around 2445 BCE (5th Dynasty) in the [[Sun Temple of Niuserre]] at [[Abusir]] ([[Abu Ghurab]]). He was also worshipped in temples at Wadi es-Sebua, [[Amada]], [[Temple of Derr|Derr]], and the [[Great Temple of Abu Simbel]], constructed by [[Ramses II]]. Amada relief of Thutmose III.jpg|[[Thutmose III]] before Ra-Horakhty in the Temple of Amada - 001 Torino Museo Egizio geroglifici su pietra.jpg|Cartouches of the god Aten (First didactic name of Aten), mentioning Ra-Horakhty in the first [[Cartouche (Egyptian hieroglyph)|cartouche]] (read from right to left) Großer Tempel (Abu Simbel) 18.jpg|Great Temple of Abu Simbel Flickr - archer10 (Dennis) - Egypt-10C-024.jpg|Great Temple of Abu Simbel, sanctuary with Ra-Horakhty, Ramses II, [[Amun-Ra]], and [[Ptah]] (right to left) Maler der Grabkammer der Nefertari 001.jpg|[[Amentet]] in the form of [[Hathor]] and Ra-Horakhty (Tomb of [[Nefertari (19th Dynasty)|Nefertari]], [[QV66]]) ## Role in the Neo-Religious Movement Thelema In [[Thelema]], a neo-religious movement founded by [[Aleister Crowley]], Ra-Horakhty is venerated under the name ''Ra-Hoor-Khuit''. He represents one of the three core principles of Thelema's teachings. ''Ra-Hoor-Khuit'' is the speaker of the third chapter of the [[The Book of the Law]], the central holy text of the movement.Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. ## See Also * [[List of Egyptian deities]] ## Literature * [[Manfred Lurker]]: ''Dictionary of Gods and Symbols of Ancient Egypt.'' Special edition, Scherz, Bern et al., 1998, ISBN 3-502-16430-4, p. 91. * Rolf Felde: ''Egyptian Deities.'' 2nd expanded and improved edition, R. Felde Eigenverlag, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * [[Hans Bonnet (Egyptologist)|Hans Bonnet]]: ''Lexicon of Egyptian Religious History.'' Nikol, Hamburg, 2000, ISBN 3-937872-08-6, pp. 269, 629–630. ## External Links {{Commonscat|Ra-Horakhty|Re-Harachte}} ## References [[Category:Egyptian deities]] [[Category:Solar deities]] [[Category:Male deities]] [[Category:Animal deities]] [[Category:Hawk deities]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[en:draft:Re-Harachte]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[de:Re-Harachte]][[Category:New pages]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Https://ar.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[category{Re-Harachte]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[it:Ra-Horakhti]] [[es:Ra-Horajty]] [[ar:%D8%B1%D8%B9-%D8%AD%D9%88%D8%B1%D8%A7%D8%AE%D8%AA%D9%8A]]

Amada relief of Thutmose III.jpg|[[Thutmose III]] before Ra-Horakhty in the Temple of Amada - 001 Torino Museo Egizio geroglifici su pietra.jpg|Cartouches of the god Aten (First didactic name of Aten), mentioning Ra-Horakhty in the first [[Cartouche (Egyptian hieroglyph)|cartouche]] (read from right to left) Großer Tempel (Abu Simbel) 18.jpg|Great Temple of Abu Simbel Flickr - archer10 (Dennis) - Egypt-10C-024.jpg|Great Temple of Abu Simbel, sanctuary with Ra-Horakhty, Ramses II, [[Amun-Ra]], and [[Ptah]] (right to left) Maler der Grabkammer der Nefertari 001.jpg|[[Amentet]] in the form of [[Hathor]] and Ra-Horakhty (Tomb of [[Nefertari (19th Dynasty)|Nefertari]], [[QV66]]) ## Role in the Neo-Religious Movement Thelema In [[Thelema]], a neo-religious movement founded by [[Aleister Crowley]], Ra-Horakhty is venerated under the name ''Ra-Hoor-Khuit''. He represents one of the three core principles of Thelema's teachings. ''Ra-Hoor-Khuit'' is the speaker of the third chapter of the [[The Book of the Law]], the central holy text of the movement.Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. ## See Also * [[List of Egyptian deities]] ## Literature * [[Manfred Lurker]]: ''Dictionary of Gods and Symbols of Ancient Egypt.'' Special edition, Scherz, Bern et al., 1998, ISBN 3-502-16430-4, p. 91. * Rolf Felde: ''Egyptian Deities.'' 2nd expanded and improved edition, R. Felde Eigenverlag, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * [[Hans Bonnet (Egyptologist)|Hans Bonnet]]: ''Lexicon of Egyptian Religious History.'' Nikol, Hamburg, 2000, ISBN 3-937872-08-6, pp. 269, 629–630. ## External Links {{Commonscat|Ra-Horakhty|Re-Harachte}} ## References [[Category:Egyptian deities]] [[Category:Solar deities]] [[Category:Male deities]] [[Category:Animal deities]] [[Category:Hawk deities]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[en:draft:Re-Harachte]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[de:Re-Harachte]][[Category:New pages]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Https://ar.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[it:Ra-Horakhti]] [[es:Ra-Horajty]] [[ar:%D8%B1%D8%B9-%D8%AD%D9%88%D8%B1%D8%A7%D8%AE%D8%AA%D9%8A]]

{{Infobox Egyptian deity |IDEOGRAM = G9 |NAME-EXTENDED = Written form |NAME = ra:Z1-G7-Axt:t*y |NAME-EXPLANATION = Ra-Horakhty
''Rˁ(w)-ḥr(.w)-3ḫ.tj''
''[[Ra (Egyptian mythology)|Ra]]-[[Horus]], Dweller of the Horizon'' |IMAGE1 = Stele Ra-Horakhty Louvre E5789.jpg |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 180px |IMAGE1-DESCRIPTION = Priest Renpetmaa with [[perfumed cone]] before Ra-Horakhty (Stele in the [[Louvre]], ca. 900 BCE, 22nd Dynasty) }} '''Ra-Horakhty''' (also spelled '''Re-Horakhty''' or '''Re-Harachte''') is a [[solar deity]] in [[Ancient Egyptian mythology]]. He emerged from the merging ([[syncretism]]) of the gods [[Ra (Egyptian mythology)|Ra]] and [[Horus of the Horizon|Horakhty]]. ## Representation Ra-Horakhty is typically depicted as a standing or seated man with red skin and a falcon head. On his head, he wears the [[sun disk]], encircled by the [[uraeus serpent]]. Other depictions show him as a man with a ram's head and sun disk, such as in the [[Mortuary Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|Temple of Seti I in Abydos]]. Additionally, he can appear with a lion's head, cat's head, or falcon head adorned with ram's horns or a sun disk, as seen in [[Hibis]]. ## Significance Ra-Horakhty is described as Ra, the solar god who rises in the east. This is the specific name of the god Ra as worshipped in [[Heliopolis (Ancient Egypt)|Heliopolis]].Rolf Felde: ''Egyptian Deities.'' Wiesbaden 1995, p. 51. In his role as the solar god of Heliopolis, he embodies the attribute of a "world ruler." Ra-Horakhty of [[Heliopolis]] was considered equivalent to the god [[Horus]] in [[Edfu]].M. Lurker: ''Dictionary of Gods and Symbols of Ancient Egypt.'' Bern et al., 1998, p. 91. [[Pharaoh]] [[Akhenaten]] referred to himself as the "High Priest of Ra-Horakhty." During the early years of his reign, he had the god depicted in his traditional form. In the first "[[Aten#The Didactic Name of Aten|didactic name]]" (Year 4–8) of the god [[Aten]], Akhenaten described him as Ra-Horakhty: “Long live the Horus of the horizon (Ra-Horakhty), who rejoices in the lightland (on the horizon).” ## Cult and Cult Centers The syncretic form of the two gods first appeared around 2445 BCE (5th Dynasty) in the [[Sun Temple of Niuserre]] at [[Abusir]] ([[Abu Ghurab]]). He was also worshipped in temples at Wadi es-Sebua, [[Amada]], [[Temple of Derr|Derr]], and the [[Great Temple of Abu Simbel]], constructed by [[Ramses II]]. Amada relief of Thutmose III.jpg|[[Thutmose III]] before Ra-Horakhty in the Temple of Amada - 001 Torino Museo Egizio geroglifici su pietra.jpg|Cartouches of the god Aten (First didactic name of Aten), mentioning Ra-Horakhty in the first [[Cartouche (Egyptian hieroglyph)|cartouche]] (read from right to left) Großer Tempel (Abu Simbel) 18.jpg|Great Temple of Abu Simbel Flickr - archer10 (Dennis) - Egypt-10C-024.jpg|Great Temple of Abu Simbel, sanctuary with Ra-Horakhty, Ramses II, [[Amun-Ra]], and [[Ptah]] (right to left) Maler der Grabkammer der Nefertari 001.jpg|[[Amentet]] in the form of [[Hathor]] and Ra-Horakhty (Tomb of [[Nefertari (19th Dynasty)|Nefertari]], [[QV66]]) ## Role in the Neo-Religious Movement Thelema In [[Thelema]], a neo-religious movement founded by [[Aleister Crowley]], Ra-Horakhty is venerated under the name ''Ra-Hoor-Khuit''. He represents one of the three core principles of Thelema's teachings. ''Ra-Hoor-Khuit'' is the speaker of the third chapter of the [[The Book of the Law]], the central holy text of the movement.Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. ## See Also * [[List of Egyptian deities]] ## Literature * [[Manfred Lurker]]: ''Dictionary of Gods and Symbols of Ancient Egypt.'' Special edition, Scherz, Bern et al., 1998, ISBN 3-502-16430-4, p. 91. * Rolf Felde: ''Egyptian Deities.'' 2nd expanded and improved edition, R. Felde Eigenverlag, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * [[Hans Bonnet (Egyptologist)|Hans Bonnet]]: ''Lexicon of Egyptian Religious History.'' Nikol, Hamburg, 2000, ISBN 3-937872-08-6, pp. 269, 629–630. ## External Links {{Commonscat|Ra-Horakhty|Re-Harachte}} ## References [[Category:Egyptian deities]] [[Category:Solar deities]] [[Category:Male deities]] [[Category:Animal deities]] [[Category:Hawk deities]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[en:draft:Re-Harachte]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[de:Re-Harachte]][[Category:New pages]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Https://ar.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[it:Ra-Horakhti]] [[es:Ra-Horajty]] [[ar:%D8%B1%D8%B9-%D8%AD%D9%88%D8%B1%D8%A7%D8%AE%D8%AA%D9%8A]]

Nikol, Hamburg, 2000, ISBN 3-937872-08-6, pp. 269, 629–630. ## External Links {{Commonscat|Ra-Horakhty|Re-Harachte}} ## References [[Category:Egyptian deities]] [[Category:Solar deities]] [[Category:Male deities]] [[Category:Animal deities]] [[Category:Hawk deities]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[en:draft:Re-Harachte]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[de:Re-Harachte]][[Category:New pages]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Https://ar.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[it:Ra-Horakhti]] [[es:Ra-Horajty]] [[ar:%D8%B1%D8%B9-%D8%AD%D9%88%D8%B1%D8%A7%D8%AE%D8%AA%D9%8A]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox Egyptian deity |IDEOGRAM = G9 |NAME-EXTENDED = Written form |NAME = ra:Z1-G7-Axt:t*y |NAME-EXPLANATION = Ra-Horakhty
''Rˁ(w)-ḥr(.w)-3ḫ.tj''
''[[Ra (Egyptian mythology)|Ra]]-[[Horus]], Dweller of the Horizon'' |IMAGE1 = Stele Ra-Horakhty Louvre E5789.jpg |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 180px |IMAGE1-DESCRIPTION = Priest Renpetmaa with [[perfumed cone]] before Ra-Horakhty (Stele in the [[Louvre]], ca. 900 BCE, 22nd Dynasty) }} '''Ra-Horakhty''' (also spelled '''Re-Horakhty''' or '''Re-Harachte''') is a [[solar deity]] in [[Ancient Egyptian mythology]]. He emerged from the merging ([[syncretism]]) of the gods [[Ra (Egyptian mythology)|Ra]] and [[Horus of the Horizon|Horakhty]]. ## Representation Ra-Horakhty is typically depicted as a standing or seated man with red skin and a falcon head. On his head, he wears the [[sun disk]], encircled by the [[uraeus serpent]]. Other depictions show him as a man with a ram's head and sun disk, such as in the [[Mortuary Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|Temple of Seti I in Abydos]]. Additionally, he can appear with a lion's head, cat's head, or falcon head adorned with ram's horns or a sun disk, as seen in [[Hibis]]. ## Significance Ra-Horakhty is described as Ra, the solar god who rises in the east. This is the specific name of the god Ra as worshipped in [[Heliopolis (Ancient Egypt)|Heliopolis]].Rolf Felde: ''Egyptian Deities.'' Wiesbaden 1995, p. 51. In his role as the solar god of Heliopolis, he embodies the attribute of a "world ruler." Ra-Horakhty of [[Heliopolis]] was considered equivalent to the god [[Horus]] in [[Edfu]].M. Lurker: ''Dictionary of Gods and Symbols of Ancient Egypt.'' Bern et al., 1998, p. 91. [[Pharaoh]] [[Akhenaten]] referred to himself as the "High Priest of Ra-Horakhty." During the early years of his reign, he had the god depicted in his traditional form. In the first "[[Aten#The Didactic Name of Aten|didactic name]]" (Year 4–8) of the god [[Aten]], Akhenaten described him as Ra-Horakhty: “Long live the Horus of the horizon (Ra-Horakhty), who rejoices in the lightland (on the horizon).” ## Cult and Cult Centers The syncretic form of the two gods first appeared around 2445 BCE (5th Dynasty) in the [[Sun Temple of Niuserre]] at [[Abusir]] ([[Abu Ghurab]]). He was also worshipped in temples at Wadi es-Sebua, [[Amada]], [[Temple of Derr|Derr]], and the [[Great Temple of Abu Simbel]], constructed by [[Ramses II]]. Amada relief of Thutmose III.jpg|[[Thutmose III]] before Ra-Horakhty in the Temple of Amada - 001 Torino Museo Egizio geroglifici su pietra.jpg|Cartouches of the god Aten (First didactic name of Aten), mentioning Ra-Horakhty in the first [[Cartouche (Egyptian hieroglyph)|cartouche]] (read from right to left) Großer Tempel (Abu Simbel) 18.jpg|Great Temple of Abu Simbel Flickr - archer10 (Dennis) - Egypt-10C-024.jpg|Great Temple of Abu Simbel, sanctuary with Ra-Horakhty, Ramses II, [[Amun-Ra]], and [[Ptah]] (right to left) Maler der Grabkammer der Nefertari 001.jpg|[[Amentet]] in the form of [[Hathor]] and Ra-Horakhty (Tomb of [[Nefertari (19th Dynasty)|Nefertari]], [[QV66]]) ## Role in the Neo-Religious Movement Thelema In [[Thelema]], a neo-religious movement founded by [[Aleister Crowley]], Ra-Horakhty is venerated under the name ''Ra-Hoor-Khuit''. He represents one of the three core principles of Thelema's teachings. ''Ra-Hoor-Khuit'' is the speaker of the third chapter of the [[The Book of the Law]], the central holy text of the movement.Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. ## See Also * [[List of Egyptian deities]] ## Literature * [[Manfred Lurker]]: ''Dictionary of Gods and Symbols of Ancient Egypt.'' Special edition, Scherz, Bern et al., 1998, ISBN 3-502-16430-4, p. 91. * Rolf Felde: ''Egyptian Deities.'' 2nd expanded and improved edition, R. Felde Eigenverlag, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * [[Hans Bonnet (Egyptologist)|Hans Bonnet]]: ''Lexicon of Egyptian Religious History.'' Nikol, Hamburg, 2000, ISBN 3-937872-08-6, pp. 269, 629–630. ## External Links {{Commonscat|Ra-Horakhty|Re-Harachte}} ## References [[Category:Egyptian deities]] [[Category:Solar deities]] [[Category:Male deities]] [[Category:Animal deities]] [[Category:Hawk deities]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[en:draft:Re-Harachte]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[de:Re-Harachte]][[Category:New pages]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Https://ar.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[it:Ra-Horakhti]] [[es:Ra-Horajty]] [[ar:%D8%B1%D8%B9-%D8%AD%D9%88%D8%B1%D8%A7%D8%AE%D8%AA%D9%8A]]

{{Infobox Egyptian deity |IDEOGRAM = G9 |NAME-EXTENDED = Written form |NAME = ra:Z1-G7-Axt:t*y |NAME-EXPLANATION = Ra-Horakhty
''Rˁ(w)-ḥr(.w)-3ḫ.tj''
''[[Ra (Egyptian mythology)|Ra]]-[[Horus]], Dweller of the Horizon'' |IMAGE1 = Stele Ra-Horakhty Louvre E5789.jpg |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 180px |IMAGE1-DESCRIPTION = Priest Renpetmaa with [[perfumed cone]] before Ra-Horakhty (Stele in the [[Louvre]], ca. 900 BCE, 22nd Dynasty) }} '''Ra-Horakhty''' (also spelled '''Re-Horakhty''' or '''Re-Harachte''') is a [[solar deity]] in [[Ancient Egyptian mythology]]. He emerged from the merging ([[syncretism]]) of the gods [[Ra (Egyptian mythology)|Ra]] and [[Horus of the Horizon|Horakhty]]. ## Representation Ra-Horakhty is typically depicted as a standing or seated man with red skin and a falcon head. On his head, he wears the [[sun disk]], encircled by the [[uraeus serpent]]. Other depictions show him as a man with a ram's head and sun disk, such as in the [[Mortuary Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|Temple of Seti I in Abydos]]. Additionally, he can appear with a lion's head, cat's head, or falcon head adorned with ram's horns or a sun disk, as seen in [[Hibis]]. ## Significance Ra-Horakhty is described as Ra, the solar god who rises in the east. This is the specific name of the god Ra as worshipped in [[Heliopolis (Ancient Egypt)|Heliopolis]].Rolf Felde: ''Egyptian Deities.'' Wiesbaden 1995, p. 51. In his role as the solar god of Heliopolis, he embodies the attribute of a "world ruler." Ra-Horakhty of [[Heliopolis]] was considered equivalent to the god [[Horus]] in [[Edfu]].M. Lurker: ''Dictionary of Gods and Symbols of Ancient Egypt.'' Bern et al., 1998, p. 91. [[Pharaoh]] [[Akhenaten]] referred to himself as the "High Priest of Ra-Horakhty." During the early years of his reign, he had the god depicted in his traditional form. In the first "[[Aten#The Didactic Name of Aten|didactic name]]" (Year 4–8) of the god [[Aten]], Akhenaten described him as Ra-Horakhty: “Long live the Horus of the horizon (Ra-Horakhty), who rejoices in the lightland (on the horizon).” ## Cult and Cult Centers The syncretic form of the two gods first appeared around 2445 BCE (5th Dynasty) in the [[Sun Temple of Niuserre]] at [[Abusir]] ([[Abu Ghurab]]). He was also worshipped in temples at Wadi es-Sebua, [[Amada]], [[Temple of Derr|Derr]], and the [[Great Temple of Abu Simbel]], constructed by [[Ramses II]]. Amada relief of Thutmose III.jpg|[[Thutmose III]] before Ra-Horakhty in the Temple of Amada - 001 Torino Museo Egizio geroglifici su pietra.jpg|Cartouches of the god Aten (First didactic name of Aten), mentioning Ra-Horakhty in the first [[Cartouche (Egyptian hieroglyph)|cartouche]] (read from right to left) Großer Tempel (Abu Simbel) 18.jpg|Great Temple of Abu Simbel Flickr - archer10 (Dennis) - Egypt-10C-024.jpg|Great Temple of Abu Simbel, sanctuary with Ra-Horakhty, Ramses II, [[Amun-Ra]], and [[Ptah]] (right to left) Maler der Grabkammer der Nefertari 001.jpg|[[Amentet]] in the form of [[Hathor]] and Ra-Horakhty (Tomb of [[Nefertari (19th Dynasty)|Nefertari]], [[QV66]]) ## Role in the Neo-Religious Movement Thelema In [[Thelema]], a neo-religious movement founded by [[Aleister Crowley]], Ra-Horakhty is venerated under the name ''Ra-Hoor-Khuit''. He represents one of the three core principles of Thelema's teachings. ''Ra-Hoor-Khuit'' is the speaker of the third chapter of the [[The Book of the Law]], the central holy text of the movement.Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. ## See Also * [[List of Egyptian deities]] ## Literature * [[Manfred Lurker]]: ''Dictionary of Gods and Symbols of Ancient Egypt.'' Special edition, Scherz, Bern et al., 1998, ISBN 3-502-16430-4, p. 91. * Rolf Felde: ''Egyptian Deities.'' 2nd expanded and improved edition, R. Felde Eigenverlag, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * [[Hans Bonnet (Egyptologist)|Hans Bonnet]]: ''Lexicon of Egyptian Religious History.'' Nikol, Hamburg, 2000, ISBN 3-937872-08-6, pp. 269, 629–630. ## External Links {{Commonscat|Ra-Horakhty|Re-Harachte}} ## References [[Category:Egyptian deities]] [[Category:Solar deities]] [[Category:Male deities]] [[Category:Animal deities]] [[Category:Hawk deities]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Re-Harachte]][[en:draft:Re-Harachte]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Re-Harachte]][[de:Re-Harachte]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:Https://ar.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[it:Ra-Horakhti]] [[es:Ra-Horajty]] [[ar:%D8%B1%D8%B9-%D8%AD%D9%88%D8%B1%D8%A7%D8%AE%D8%AA%D9%8A]]

In the first "[[Aten#The Didactic Name of Aten|didactic name]]" (Year 4–8) of the god [[Aten]], Akhenaten described him as Ra-Horakhty: “Long live the Horus of the horizon (Ra-Horakhty), who rejoices in the lightland (on the horizon).” ## Cult and Cult Centers The syncretic form of the two gods first appeared around 2445 BCE (5th Dynasty) in the [[Sun Temple of Niuserre]] at [[Abusir]] ([[Abu Ghurab]]). He was also worshipped in temples at Wadi es-Sebua, [[Amada]], [[Temple of Derr|Derr]], and the [[Great Temple of Abu Simbel]], constructed by [[Ramses II]]. Amada relief of Thutmose III.jpg|[[Thutmose III]] before Ra-Horakhty in the Temple of Amada - 001 Torino Museo Egizio geroglifici su pietra.jpg|Cartouches of the god Aten (First didactic name of Aten), mentioning Ra-Horakhty in the first [[Cartouche (Egyptian hieroglyph)|cartouche]] (read from right to left) Großer Tempel (Abu Simbel) 18.jpg|Great Temple of Abu Simbel Flickr - archer10 (Dennis) - Egypt-10C-024.jpg|Great Temple of Abu Simbel, sanctuary with Ra-Horakhty, Ramses II, [[Amun-Ra]], and [[Ptah]] (right to left) Maler der Grabkammer der Nefertari 001.jpg|[[Amentet]] in the form of [[Hathor]] and Ra-Horakhty (Tomb of [[Nefertari (19th Dynasty)|Nefertari]], [[QV66]]) ## Role in the Neo-Religious Movement Thelema In [[Thelema]], a neo-religious movement founded by [[Aleister Crowley]], Ra-Horakhty is venerated under the name ''Ra-Hoor-Khuit''. He represents one of the three core principles of Thelema's teachings. ''Ra-Hoor-Khuit'' is the speaker of the third chapter of the [[The Book of the Law]], the central holy text of the movement.Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. ## See Also * [[List of Egyptian deities]] ## Literature * [[Manfred Lurker]]: ''Dictionary of Gods and Symbols of Ancient Egypt.'' Special edition, Scherz, Bern et al., 1998, ISBN 3-502-16430-4, p. 91. * Rolf Felde: ''Egyptian Deities.'' 2nd expanded and improved edition, R. Felde Eigenverlag, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * [[Hans Bonnet (Egyptologist)|Hans Bonnet]]: ''Lexicon of Egyptian Religious History.'' Nikol, Hamburg, 2000, ISBN 3-937872-08-6, pp. 269, 629–630. ## External Links {{Commonscat|Ra-Horakhty|Re-Harachte}} ## References [[Category:Egyptian deities]] [[Category:Solar deities]] [[Category:Male deities]] [[Category:Animal deities]] [[Category:Hawk deities]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Re-Harachte-qq]] [[Category:Re-Harachte]][[en:draft:Re-Harachte]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Re-Harachte-qq]] [[Category:Re-Harachte]][[de:Re-Harachte]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Re-Harachte-qq]] [[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:Https://ar.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Re-Harachte-qq]] [[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:Re-Harachte]][[it:Ra-Horakhti]] [[es:Ra-Horajty]] [[ar:%D8%B1%D8%B9-%D8%AD%D9%88%D8%B1%D8%A7%D8%AE%D8%AA%D9%8A]]

{{Infobox Egyptian deity |IDEOGRAM = G9 |NAME-EXTENDED = Written form |NAME = ra:Z1-G7-Axt:t*y |NAME-EXPLANATION = Ra-Horakhty
''Rˁ(w)-ḥr(.w)-3ḫ.tj''
''[[Ra (Egyptian mythology)|Ra]]-[[Horus]], Dweller of the Horizon'' |IMAGE1 = Stele Ra-Horakhty Louvre E5789.jpg |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 180px |IMAGE1-DESCRIPTION = Priest Renpetmaa with [[perfumed cone]] before Ra-Horakhty (Stele in the [[Louvre]], ca. 900 BCE, 22nd Dynasty) }} '''Ra-Horakhty''' (also spelled '''Re-Horakhty''' or '''Re-Harachte''') is a [[solar deity]] in [[Ancient Egyptian mythology]]. He emerged from the merging ([[syncretism]]) of the gods [[Ra (Egyptian mythology)|Ra]] and [[Horus of the Horizon|Horakhty]]. ## Representation Ra-Horakhty is typically depicted as a standing or seated man with red skin and a falcon head. On his head, he wears the [[sun disk]], encircled by the [[uraeus serpent]]. Other depictions show him as a man with a ram's head and sun disk, such as in the [[Mortuary Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|Temple of Seti I in Abydos]]. Additionally, he can appear with a lion's head, cat's head, or falcon head adorned with ram's horns or a sun disk, as seen in [[Hibis]]. ## Significance Ra-Horakhty is described as Ra, the solar god who rises in the east. This is the specific name of the god Ra as worshipped in [[Heliopolis (Ancient Egypt)|Heliopolis]].Rolf Felde: ''Egyptian Deities.'' Wiesbaden 1995, p. 51. In his role as the solar god of Heliopolis, he embodies the attribute of a "world ruler." Ra-Horakhty of [[Heliopolis]] was considered equivalent to the god [[Horus]] in [[Edfu]].M. Lurker: ''Dictionary of Gods and Symbols of Ancient Egypt.'' Bern et al., 1998, p. 91. [[Pharaoh]] [[Akhenaten]] referred to himself as the "High Priest of Ra-Horakhty." During the early years of his reign, he had the god depicted in his traditional form. In the first "[[Aten#The Didactic Name of Aten|didactic name]]" (Year 4–8) of the god [[Aten]], Akhenaten described him as Ra-Horakhty: “Long live the Horus of the horizon (Ra-Horakhty), who rejoices in the lightland (on the horizon).” ## Cult and Cult Centers The syncretic form of the two gods first appeared around 2445 BCE (5th Dynasty) in the [[Sun Temple of Niuserre]] at [[Abusir]] ([[Abu Ghurab]]). He was also worshipped in temples at Wadi es-Sebua, [[Amada]], [[Temple of Derr|Derr]], and the [[Great Temple of Abu Simbel]], constructed by [[Ramses II]]. Amada relief of Thutmose III.jpg|[[Thutmose III]] before Ra-Horakhty in the Temple of Amada - 001 Torino Museo Egizio geroglifici su pietra.jpg|Cartouches of the god Aten (First didactic name of Aten), mentioning Ra-Horakhty in the first [[Cartouche (Egyptian hieroglyph)|cartouche]] (read from right to left) Großer Tempel (Abu Simbel) 18.jpg|Great Temple of Abu Simbel Flickr - archer10 (Dennis) - Egypt-10C-024.jpg|Great Temple of Abu Simbel, sanctuary with Ra-Horakhty, Ramses II, [[Amun-Ra]], and [[Ptah]] (right to left) Maler der Grabkammer der Nefertari 001.jpg|[[Amentet]] in the form of [[Hathor]] and Ra-Horakhty (Tomb of [[Nefertari (19th Dynasty)|Nefertari]], [[QV66]]) ## Role in the Neo-Religious Movement Thelema In [[Thelema]], a neo-religious movement founded by [[Aleister Crowley]], Ra-Horakhty is venerated under the name ''Ra-Hoor-Khuit''. He represents one of the three core principles of Thelema's teachings. ''Ra-Hoor-Khuit'' is the speaker of the third chapter of the [[The Book of the Law]], the central holy text of the movement.Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. ## See Also * [[List of Egyptian deities]] ## Literature * [[Manfred Lurker]]: ''Dictionary of Gods and Symbols of Ancient Egypt.'' Special edition, Scherz, Bern et al., 1998, ISBN 3-502-16430-4, p. 91. * Rolf Felde: ''Egyptian Deities.'' 2nd expanded and improved edition, R. Felde Eigenverlag, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * [[Hans Bonnet (Egyptologist)|Hans Bonnet]]: ''Lexicon of Egyptian Religious History.'' Nikol, Hamburg, 2000, ISBN 3-937872-08-6, pp. 269, 629–630. ## External Links {{Commonscat|Ra-Horakhty|Re-Harachte}} ## References [[Category:Egyptian deities]] [[Category:Solar deities]] [[Category:Male deities]] [[Category:Animal deities]] [[Category:Hawk deities]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Re-Harachte-qq]] [[Category:Re-Harachte]][[en:draft:Re-Harachte]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Re-Harachte-qq]] [[Category:Re-Harachte]][[de:Re-Harachte]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Re-Harachte-qq]] [[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:Https://ar.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Re-Harachte-qq]] [[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:Re-Harachte]][[it:Ra-Horakhti]] [[es:Ra-Horajty]] [[ar:%D8%B1%D8%B9-%D8%AD%D9%88%D8%B1%D8%A7%D8%AE%D8%AA%D9%8A]]

''Ra-Hoor-Khuit'' is the speaker of the third chapter of the [[The Book of the Law]], the central holy text of the movement.Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' {{Infobox Egyptian deity |IDEOGRAM = G9 |NAME-EXTENDED = Written form |NAME = ra:Z1-G7-Axt:t*y |NAME-EXPLANATION = Ra-Horakhty
''Rˁ(w)-ḥr(.w)-3ḫ.tj''
''[[Ra (Egyptian mythology)|Ra]]-[[Horus]], Dweller of the Horizon'' |IMAGE1 = Stele Ra-Horakhty Louvre E5789.jpg |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 180px |IMAGE1-DESCRIPTION = Priest Renpetmaa with [[perfumed cone]] before Ra-Horakhty (Stele in the [[Louvre]], ca. 900 BCE, 22nd Dynasty) }} '''Ra-Horakhty''' (also spelled '''Re-Horakhty''' or '''Re-Harachte''') is a [[solar deity]] in [[Ancient Egyptian mythology]]. He emerged from the merging ([[syncretism]]) of the gods [[Ra (Egyptian mythology)|Ra]] and [[Horus of the Horizon|Horakhty]]. ## Representation Ra-Horakhty is typically depicted as a standing or seated man with red skin and a falcon head. On his head, he wears the [[sun disk]], encircled by the [[uraeus serpent]]. Other depictions show him as a man with a ram's head and sun disk, such as in the [[Mortuary Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|Temple of Seti I in Abydos]]. Additionally, he can appear with a lion's head, cat's head, or falcon head adorned with ram's horns or a sun disk, as seen in [[Hibis]]. ## Significance Ra-Horakhty is described as Ra, the solar god who rises in the east. This is the specific name of the god Ra as worshipped in [[Heliopolis (Ancient Egypt)|Heliopolis]].Rolf Felde: ''Egyptian Deities.'' Wiesbaden 1995, p. 51. In his role as the solar god of Heliopolis, he embodies the attribute of a "world ruler." Ra-Horakhty of [[Heliopolis]] was considered equivalent to the god [[Horus]] in [[Edfu]].M. Lurker: ''Dictionary of Gods and Symbols of Ancient Egypt.'' Bern et al., 1998, p. 91. [[Pharaoh]] [[Akhenaten]] referred to himself as the "High Priest of Ra-Horakhty." During the early years of his reign, he had the god depicted in his traditional form. In the first "[[Aten#The Didactic Name of Aten|didactic name]]" (Year 4–8) of the god [[Aten]], Akhenaten described him as Ra-Horakhty: “Long live the Horus of the horizon (Ra-Horakhty), who rejoices in the lightland (on the horizon).” ## Cult and Cult Centers The syncretic form of the two gods first appeared around 2445 BCE (5th Dynasty) in the [[Sun Temple of Niuserre]] at [[Abusir]] ([[Abu Ghurab]]). He was also worshipped in temples at Wadi es-Sebua, [[Amada]], [[Temple of Derr|Derr]], and the [[Great Temple of Abu Simbel]], constructed by [[Ramses II]]. He represents one of the three core principles of Thelema's teachings. ''Ra-Hoor-Khuit'' is the speaker of the third chapter of the [[The Book of the Law]], the central holy text of the movement.Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. ## See Also * [[List of Egyptian deities]] ## Literature * [[Manfred Lurker]]: ''Dictionary of Gods and Symbols of Ancient Egypt.'' Special edition, Scherz, Bern et al., 1998, ISBN 3-502-16430-4, p. 91. * Rolf Felde: ''Egyptian Deities.'' 2nd expanded and improved edition, R. Felde Eigenverlag, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * [[Hans Bonnet (Egyptologist)|Hans Bonnet]]: ''Lexicon of Egyptian Religious History.'' Nikol, Hamburg, 2000, ISBN 3-937872-08-6, pp. 269, 629–630. ## External Links {{Commonscat|Ra-Horakhty|Re-Harachte}} ## References [[Category:Egyptian deities]] [[Category:Solar deities]] [[Category:Male deities]] [[Category:Animal deities]] [[Category:Hawk deities]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Re-Harachte-qq]] [[Category:Re-Harachte]][[en:draft:Re-Harachte]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Re-Harachte-qq]] [[Category:Re-Harachte]][[de:Re-Harachte]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Re-Harachte-qq]] [[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:Https://ar.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Re-Harachte-qq]] [[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:Re-Harachte]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]][[sn:Re-Harachte]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Re-Harachte]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Re-Harachte]][[Category:qq]] [[it:Ra-Horakhti]] [[es:Ra-Horajty]] [[ar:%D8%B1%D8%B9-%D8%AD%D9%88%D8%B1%D8%A7%D8%AE%D8%AA%D9%8A]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Re-Harachte]][[Category:qq]]

Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. ## See Also * [[List of Egyptian deities]] ## Literature * [[Manfred Lurker]]: ''Dictionary of Gods and Symbols of Ancient Egypt.'' Special edition, Scherz, Bern et al., 1998, ISBN 3-502-16430-4, p. 91. * Rolf Felde: ''Egyptian Deities.'' 2nd expanded and improved edition, R. Felde Eigenverlag, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * [[Hans Bonnet (Egyptologist)|Hans Bonnet]]: ''Lexicon of Egyptian Religious History.'' Nikol, Hamburg, 2000, ISBN 3-937872-08-6, pp. 269, 629–630. ## External Links {{Commonscat|Ra-Horakhty|Re-Harachte}} ## References [[Category:Egyptian deities]] [[Category:Solar deities]] [[Category:Male deities]] [[Category:Animal deities]] [[Category:Hawk deities]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Re-Harachte-qq]] [[Category:Re-Harachte]][[en:draft:Re-Harachte]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Re-Harachte-qq]] [[Category:Re-Harachte]][[de:Re-Harachte]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Re-Harachte-qq]] [[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:Https://ar.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Re-Harachte-qq]] [[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:Re-Harachte]] [[Category:suQqqqqq]][[sn:Re-Harachte]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Re-Harachte]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Re-Harachte]][[Category:qq]] [[it:Ra-Horakhti]] [[es:Ra-Horajty]] [[ar:%D8%B1%D8%B9-%D8%AD%D9%88%D8%B1%D8%A7%D8%AE%D8%AA%D9%8A]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Re-Harachte]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Re-Harachte]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]]

{{Infobox Egyptian deity |IDEOGRAM = G9 |NAME-EXTENDED = Written form |NAME = ra:Z1-G7-Axt:t*y |NAME-EXPLANATION = Ra-Horakhty
''Rˁ(w)-ḥr(.w)-3ḫ.tj''
''[[Ra (Egyptian mythology)|Ra]]-[[Horus]], Dweller of the Horizon'' |IMAGE1 = Stele Ra-Horakhty Louvre E5789.jpg |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 180px |IMAGE1-DESCRIPTION = Priest Renpetmaa with [[perfumed cone]] before Ra-Horakhty (Stele in the [[Louvre]], ca. 900 BCE, 22nd Dynasty) }} '''Ra-Horakhty''' (also spelled '''Re-Horakhty''' or '''Re-Harachte''') is a [[solar deity]] in [[Ancient Egyptian mythology]]. He emerged from the merging ([[syncretism]]) of the gods [[Ra (Egyptian mythology)|Ra]] and [[Horus of the Horizon|Horakhty]]. ## Representation Ra-Horakhty is typically depicted as a standing or seated man with red skin and a falcon head. On his head, he wears the [[sun disk]], encircled by the [[uraeus serpent]]. Other depictions show him as a man with a ram's head and sun disk, such as in the [[Mortuary Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|Temple of Seti I in Abydos]]. Additionally, he can appear with a lion's head, cat's head, or falcon head adorned with ram's horns or a sun disk, as seen in [[Hibis]]. ## Significance Ra-Horakhty is described as Ra, the solar god who rises in the east. This is the specific name of the god Ra as worshipped in [[Heliopolis (Ancient Egypt)|Heliopolis]].Rolf Felde: ''Egyptian Deities.'' Wiesbaden 1995, p. 51. In his role as the solar god of Heliopolis, he embodies the attribute of a "world ruler." Ra-Horakhty of [[Heliopolis]] was considered equivalent to the god [[Horus]] in [[Edfu]].M. Lurker: ''Dictionary of Gods and Symbols of Ancient Egypt.'' Bern et al., 1998, p. 91. [[Pharaoh]] [[Akhenaten]] referred to himself as the "High Priest of Ra-Horakhty." During the early years of his reign, he had the god depicted in his traditional form. In the first "[[Aten#The Didactic Name of Aten|didactic name]]" (Year 4–8) of the god [[Aten]], Akhenaten described him as Ra-Horakhty: “Long live the Horus of the horizon (Ra-Horakhty), who rejoices in the lightland (on the horizon).” ## Cult and Cult Centers The syncretic form of the two gods first appeared around 2445 BCE (5th Dynasty) in the [[Sun Temple of Niuserre]] at [[Abusir]] ([[Abu Ghurab]]). He was also worshipped in temples at Wadi es-Sebua, [[Amada]], [[Temple of Derr|Derr]], and the [[Great Temple of Abu Simbel]], constructed by [[Ramses II]]. Amada relief of Thutmose III.jpg|[[Thutmose III]] before Ra-Horakhty in the Temple of Amada - 001 Torino Museo Egizio geroglifici su pietra.jpg|Cartouches of the god Aten (First didactic name of Aten), mentioning Ra-Horakhty in the first [[Cartouche (Egyptian hieroglyph)|cartouche]] (read from right to left) Großer Tempel (Abu Simbel) 18.jpg|Great Temple of Abu Simbel Flickr - archer10 (Dennis) - Egypt-10C-024.jpg|Great Temple of Abu Simbel, sanctuary with Ra-Horakhty, Ramses II, [[Amun-Ra]], and [[Ptah]] (right to left) Maler der Grabkammer der Nefertari 001.jpg|[[Amentet]] in the form of [[Hathor]] and Ra-Horakhty (Tomb of [[Nefertari (19th Dynasty)|Nefertari]], [[QV66]]) ## Role in the Neo-Religious Movement Thelema In [[Thelema]], a neo-religious movement founded by [[Aleister Crowley]], Ra-Horakhty is venerated under the name ''Ra-Hoor-Khuit''. He represents one of the three core principles of Thelema's teachings. ''Ra-Hoor-Khuit'' is the speaker of the third chapter of the [[The Book of the Law]], the central holy text of the movement.Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. ## See Also * [[List of Egyptian deities]] ## Literature * [[Manfred Lurker]]: ''Dictionary of Gods and Symbols of Ancient Egypt.'' Special edition, Scherz, Bern et al., 1998, ISBN 3-502-16430-4, p. 91. * Rolf Felde: ''Egyptian Deities.'' 2nd expanded and improved edition, R. Felde Eigenverlag, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * [[Hans Bonnet (Egyptologist)|Hans Bonnet]]: ''Lexicon of Egyptian Religious History.'' Nikol, Hamburg, 2000, ISBN 3-937872-08-6, pp. 269, 629–630. ## External Links {{Commonscat|Ra-Horakhty|Re-Harachte}} ## References [[Category:Egyptian deities]] [[Category:Solar deities]] [[Category:Male deities]] [[Category:Animal deities]] [[Category:Hawk deities]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Re-Harachte-qq]] [[Category:Re-Harachte]][[en:draft:Re-Harachte]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Re-Harachte-qq]] [[Category:Re-Harachte]][[de:Re-Harachte]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Re-Harachte-qq]] [[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:Https://ar.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Re-Harachte-qq]] [[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Re-Harachte]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:Re-Harachte]] [[Category:suQqqqqq]][[sn:Re-Harachte]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Re-Harachte]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Re-Harachte]][[Category:qq]] [[it:Ra-Horakhti]] [[es:Ra-Horajty]] [[ar:%D8%B1%D8%B9-%D8%AD%D9%88%D8%B1%D8%A7%D8%AE%D8%AA%D9%8A]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Re-Harachte]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Re-Harachte]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]]

''{{ill|Ra (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)}}-{{ill|Horus|de|Horus}}, Dweller of the Horizon'' |IMAGE1 = Stele Ra-Horakhty Louvre E5789.jpg |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 180px |IMAGE1-CAPTION = Priest Renpetmaa with {{ill|Unguent cone|de|Salbkegel}} before Re-Harakhte (Stele in the {{ill|Louvre|de|Louvre}}, ca. 900 BCE, {{ill|22nd Dynasty of Egypt|de|22. Dynastie}}) }} '''Re-Harakhte''' (also spelled '''Re-Horakhty''', '''Re-Hor-achti''') is a {{ill|solar deity|de|Sonnengott}} in {{ill|Egyptian mythology|de|Ägyptische Mythologie}}. This god emerged through the fusion ({{ill|Syncretism (Egyptology)|de|Synkretismus (Ägyptologie)}}) of {{ill|Ra (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)}} and {{ill|Harakhti|de|Harachte}}. == Depiction == Re-Harakhte is typically portrayed as a **standing or seated man** with **red skin** and a **falcon head**. On his head, he wears the **{{ill|solar disk|de|Sonnenscheibe}}**, encircled by the **{{ill|Uraeus serpent|de|Uräusschlange (Symbol)}}**. Other representations depict him as a **human with a ram's head** and a **solar disk**, such as in the **{{ill|Mortuary Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|de|Totentempel des Sethos I. (Abydos)}}**, or as having a **lion, cat, or falcon head with ram horns and a solar disk**, particularly in **({{ill|Hibis|de|Hibis}}).** == Meaning == Re-Harakhte describes Ra as the **rising sun in the east.** It is the actual name of the god **Ra of Heliopolis.**Rolf Felde: ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' Wiesbaden, 1995, p. 51. As the **Heliopolitan sun god**, he is highlighted as the **"ruler of the world."** In {{ill|Edfu|de|Edfu}}, **Re-Harakhte of Heliopolis** was regarded as **identical to the god {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}}.**M. Lurker: ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Bern, 1998, p. 91. {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao}} **{{ill|Akhenaten|de|Echnaton}}** called himself the **"High Priest of Re-Harakhte"**, and at the start of his reign, he depicted the god in his traditional form. However, in **the first so-called "{{ill|Didactic name of Aten|de|Aton#Der lehrhafte Name des Aton}}"** (Years 4–8 of his reign), he described **Aten** as **Re-Harakhte**: *"Long live the **Horizon Horus** (Re-Harakhte), who rejoices in the Light Land (Horizon)."* == Cult and Worship Sites == As early as **2445 BCE** ({{ill|5th Dynasty of Egypt|de|5. Dynastie}}), the **fusion form of these two gods** appeared in the **{{ill|Sun Temple of Nyuserre|de|Sonnenheiligtum des Niuserre}}** at **{{ill|Abusir|de|Abusir}}** (**{{ill|Abu Ghurab|de|Abu Ghurab}}**). Re-Harakhte was also worshiped in temples at: - **Wadi es-Sebua** - **{{ill|Amada (Nubian village)|de|Amada (nubisches Dorf)}}** - **{{ill|Temple of Derr|de|Tempel von Derr}}** - The **{{ill|Great Temple of Abu Simbel|de|Tempel von Abu Simbel}}**, built by **{{ill|Ramesses II|de|Ramses II.}}** Amada relief of Thutmose III.jpg|{{ill|Thutmose III|de|Thutmosis III.}} in the temple of **Amada** before **Re-Harakhte** - 001 Torino Museo Egizio geroglifici su pietra.jpg|Cartouches of **Aten** (First didactic name of Aten) mentioning **Re-Harakhte** in the first **{{ill|cartouche (Ancient Egypt)|de|Kartusche (Altes Ägypten)}}** (read from right to left) Großer Tempel (Abu Simbel) 18.jpg|**Great Temple of Abu Simbel** Flickr - archer10 (Dennis) - Egypt-10C-024.jpg|**Great Temple of Abu Simbel**, sanctuary with **Re-Harakhte, Ramesses II, {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re}}, and {{ill|Ptah|de|Ptah}}** (from right to left) Maler der Grabkammer der Nefertari 001.jpg|**{{ill|Amentet|de|Amentet}}** in the form of **{{ill|Hathor|de|Hathor}}** with **Re-Harakhte** (Tomb of **{{ill|Nefertari (19th Dynasty)|de|Nefertari (19. Dynastie)}}**, **{{ill|QV66|de|QV66}}**) == Reception in the New Religious Movement Thelema == In **{{ill|Thelema|de|Thelema}}**, a **new religious movement founded by {{ill|Aleister Crowley|de|Aleister Crowley}}**, Re-Harakhte is known as **"Ra-Hoor-Khuit"** and represents **one of the three central principles of Thelemic doctrine.** "Ra-Hoor-Khuit" appears as the speaker of the **third chapter** of **{{ill|Liber AL vel Legis|de|Liber AL vel Legis}}**, the **sacred text of Thelema.**Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian deities|de|Liste ägyptischer Götter}} == References == * **Manfred Lurker**: ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Special edition, Scherz, Bern, 1998, ISBN 3-502-16430-4, p. 91. * **Rolf Felde**: ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' 2nd expanded and improved edition, R. Felde Publishing, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * **Hans Bonnet**: ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Religionsgeschichte.'' {{Infobox Egyptian Deity |IDEOGRAM = G9 |NAME-EXTENDED = Fully written out |NAME = ra:Z1-G7-Axt:t*y |NAME-EXPLANATION = Ra(u)-heru-achti
''Rˁ(w)-ḥr(.w)-3ḫ.tj''
''{{ill|Ra (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)}}-{{ill|Horus|de|Horus}}, Dweller of the Horizon'' |IMAGE1 = Stele Ra-Horakhty Louvre E5789.jpg |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 180px |IMAGE1-CAPTION = Priest Renpetmaa with {{ill|Unguent cone|de|Salbkegel}} before Re-Harakhte (Stele in the {{ill|Louvre|de|Louvre}}, ca. 900 BCE, {{ill|22nd Dynasty of Egypt|de|22. Dynastie}}) }} '''Re-Harakhte''' (also spelled '''Re-Horakhty''', '''Re-Hor-achti''') is a {{ill|solar deity|de|Sonnengott}} in {{ill|Egyptian mythology|de|Ägyptische Mythologie}}. This god emerged through the fusion ({{ill|Syncretism (Egyptology)|de|Synkretismus (Ägyptologie)}}) of {{ill|Ra (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)}} and {{ill|Harakhti|de|Harachte}}. == Depiction == Re-Harakhte is typically portrayed as a **standing or seated man** with **red skin** and a **falcon head**. On his head, he wears the **{{ill|solar disk|de|Sonnenscheibe}}**, encircled by the **{{ill|Uraeus serpent|de|Uräusschlange (Symbol)}}**. Other representations depict him as a **human with a ram's head** and a **solar disk**, such as in the **{{ill|Mortuary Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|de|Totentempel des Sethos I. (Abydos)}}**, or as having a **lion, cat, or falcon head with ram horns and a solar disk**, particularly in **({{ill|Hibis|de|Hibis}}).** == Meaning == Re-Harakhte describes Ra as the **rising sun in the east.** It is the actual name of the god **Ra of Heliopolis.**Rolf Felde: ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' Wiesbaden, 1995, p. 51. As the **Heliopolitan sun god**, he is highlighted as the **"ruler of the world."** In {{ill|Edfu|de|Edfu}}, **Re-Harakhte of Heliopolis** was regarded as **identical to the god {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}}.**M. Lurker: ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Bern, 1998, p. 91. {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao}} **{{ill|Akhenaten|de|Echnaton}}** called himself the **"High Priest of Re-Harakhte"**, and at the start of his reign, he depicted the god in his traditional form. However, in **the first so-called "{{ill|Didactic name of Aten|de|Aton#Der lehrhafte Name des Aton}}"** (Years 4–8 of his reign), he described **Aten** as **Re-Harakhte**: *"Long live the **Horizon Horus** (Re-Harakhte), who rejoices in the Light Land (Horizon)."* == Cult and Worship Sites == As early as **2445 BCE** ({{ill|5th Dynasty of Egypt|de|5. Dynastie}}), the **fusion form of these two gods** appeared in the **{{ill|Sun Temple of Nyuserre|de|Sonnenheiligtum des Niuserre}}** at **{{ill|Abusir|de|Abusir}}** (**{{ill|Abu Ghurab|de|Abu Ghurab}}**). Re-Harakhte was also worshiped in temples at: - **Wadi es-Sebua** - **{{ill|Amada (Nubian village)|de|Amada (nubisches Dorf)}}** - **{{ill|Temple of Derr|de|Tempel von Derr}}** - The **{{ill|Great Temple of Abu Simbel|de|Tempel von Abu Simbel}}**, built by **{{ill|Ramesses II|de|Ramses II.}}** Amada relief of Thutmose III.jpg|{{ill|Thutmose III|de|Thutmosis III.}} in the temple of **Amada** before **Re-Harakhte** - 001 Torino Museo Egizio geroglifici su pietra.jpg|Cartouches of **Aten** (First didactic name of Aten) mentioning **Re-Harakhte** in the first **{{ill|cartouche (Ancient Egypt)|de|Kartusche (Altes Ägypten)}}** (read from right to left) Großer Tempel (Abu Simbel) 18.jpg|**Great Temple of Abu Simbel** Flickr - archer10 (Dennis) - Egypt-10C-024.jpg|**Great Temple of Abu Simbel**, sanctuary with **Re-Harakhte, Ramesses II, {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re}}, and {{ill|Ptah|de|Ptah}}** (from right to left) Maler der Grabkammer der Nefertari 001.jpg|**{{ill|Amentet|de|Amentet}}** in the form of **{{ill|Hathor|de|Hathor}}** with **Re-Harakhte** (Tomb of **{{ill|Nefertari (19th Dynasty)|de|Nefertari (19. Dynastie)}}**, **{{ill|QV66|de|QV66}}**) == Reception in the New Religious Movement Thelema == In **{{ill|Thelema|de|Thelema}}**, a **new religious movement founded by {{ill|Aleister Crowley|de|Aleister Crowley}}**, Re-Harakhte is known as **"Ra-Hoor-Khuit"** and represents **one of the three central principles of Thelemic doctrine.** "Ra-Hoor-Khuit" appears as the speaker of the **third chapter** of **{{ill|Liber AL vel Legis|de|Liber AL vel Legis}}**, the **sacred text of Thelema.**Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian deities|de|Liste ägyptischer Götter}} == References == * **Manfred Lurker**: ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Special edition, Scherz, Bern, 1998, ISBN 3-502-16430-4, p. 91. * **Rolf Felde**: ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' 2nd expanded and improved edition, R. Felde Publishing, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * **Hans Bonnet**: ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Religionsgeschichte.'' Nikol, Hamburg, 2000, ISBN 3-937872-08-6, pp. 269, 629–630. == External links == {{Commonscat|Ra-Horakhty|Re-Harakhte}} == Notes == [[Category:Egyptian deities]] [[Category:Solar deities]] Re-Harakhte was also worshiped in temples at: - **Wadi es-Sebua** - **{{ill|Amada (Nubian village)|de|Amada (nubisches Dorf)}}** - **{{ill|Temple of Derr|de|Tempel von Derr}}** - The **{{ill|Great Temple of Abu Simbel|de|Tempel von Abu Simbel}}**, built by **{{ill|Ramesses II|de|Ramses II.}}** Amada relief of Thutmose III.jpg|{{ill|Thutmose III|de|Thutmosis III.}} in the temple of **Amada** before **Re-Harakhte** - 001 Torino Museo Egizio geroglifici su pietra.jpg|Cartouches of **Aten** (First didactic name of Aten) mentioning **Re-Harakhte** in the first **{{ill|cartouche (Ancient Egypt)|de|Kartusche (Altes Ägypten)}}** (read from right to left) Großer Tempel (Abu Simbel) 18.jpg|**Great Temple of Abu Simbel** Flickr - archer10 (Dennis) - Egypt-10C-024.jpg|**Great Temple of Abu Simbel**, sanctuary with **Re-Harakhte, Ramesses II, {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re}}, and {{ill|Ptah|de|Ptah}}** (from right to left) Maler der Grabkammer der Nefertari 001.jpg|**{{ill|Amentet|de|Amentet}}** in the form of **{{ill|Hathor|de|Hathor}}** with **Re-Harakhte** (Tomb of **{{ill|Nefertari (19th Dynasty)|de|Nefertari (19. Dynastie)}}**, **{{ill|QV66|de|QV66}}**) == Reception in the New Religious Movement Thelema == In **{{ill|Thelema|de|Thelema}}**, a **new religious movement founded by {{ill|Aleister Crowley|de|Aleister Crowley}}**, Re-Harakhte is known as **"Ra-Hoor-Khuit"** and represents **one of the three central principles of Thelemic doctrine.** "Ra-Hoor-Khuit" appears as the speaker of the **third chapter** of **{{ill|Liber AL vel Legis|de|Liber AL vel Legis}}**, the **sacred text of Thelema.**Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian deities|de|Liste ägyptischer Götter}} == References == * **Manfred Lurker**: ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' {{Infobox Egyptian Deity |IDEOGRAM = G9 |NAME-EXTENDED = Fully written out |NAME = ra:Z1-G7-Axt:t*y |NAME-EXPLANATION = Ra(u)-heru-achti
''Rˁ(w)-ḥr(.w)-3ḫ.tj''
''{{ill|Ra (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)}}-{{ill|Horus|de|Horus}}, Dweller of the Horizon'' |IMAGE1 = Stele Ra-Horakhty Louvre E5789.jpg |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 180px |IMAGE1-CAPTION = Priest Renpetmaa with {{ill|Unguent cone|de|Salbkegel}} before Re-Harakhte (Stele in the {{ill|Louvre|de|Louvre}}, ca. 900 BCE, {{ill|22nd Dynasty of Egypt|de|22. Dynastie}}) }} '''Re-Harakhte''' (also spelled '''Re-Horakhty''', '''Re-Hor-achti''') is a {{ill|solar deity|de|Sonnengott}} in {{ill|Egyptian mythology|de|Ägyptische Mythologie}}. This god emerged through the fusion ({{ill|Syncretism (Egyptology)|de|Synkretismus (Ägyptologie)}}) of {{ill|Ra (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)}} and {{ill|Harakhti|de|Harachte}}. == Depiction == Re-Harakhte is typically portrayed as a **standing or seated man** with **red skin** and a **falcon head**. On his head, he wears the **{{ill|solar disk|de|Sonnenscheibe}}**, encircled by the **{{ill|Uraeus serpent|de|Uräusschlange (Symbol)}}**. Other representations depict him as a **human with a ram's head** and a **solar disk**, such as in the **{{ill|Mortuary Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|de|Totentempel des Sethos I. (Abydos)}}**, or as having a **lion, cat, or falcon head with ram horns and a solar disk**, particularly in **({{ill|Hibis|de|Hibis}}).** == Meaning == Re-Harakhte describes Ra as the **rising sun in the east.** It is the actual name of the god **Ra of Heliopolis.**Rolf Felde: ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' Wiesbaden, 1995, p. 51. As the **Heliopolitan sun god**, he is highlighted as the **"ruler of the world."** In {{ill|Edfu|de|Edfu}}, **Re-Harakhte of Heliopolis** was regarded as **identical to the god {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}}.**M. Lurker: ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Bern, 1998, p. 91. {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao}} **{{ill|Akhenaten|de|Echnaton}}** called himself the **"High Priest of Re-Harakhte"**, and at the start of his reign, he depicted the god in his traditional form. However, in **the first so-called "{{ill|Didactic name of Aten|de|Aton#Der lehrhafte Name des Aton}}"** (Years 4–8 of his reign), he described **Aten** as **Re-Harakhte**: *"Long live the **Horizon Horus** (Re-Harakhte), who rejoices in the Light Land (Horizon)."* == Cult and Worship Sites == As early as **2445 BCE** ({{ill|5th Dynasty of Egypt|de|5. Dynastie}}), the **fusion form of these two gods** appeared in the **{{ill|Sun Temple of Nyuserre|de|Sonnenheiligtum des Niuserre}}** at **{{ill|Abusir|de|Abusir}}** (**{{ill|Abu Ghurab|de|Abu Ghurab}}**). Re-Harakhte was also worshiped in temples at: - **Wadi es-Sebua** - **{{ill|Amada (Nubian village)|de|Amada (nubisches Dorf)}}** - **{{ill|Temple of Derr|de|Tempel von Derr}}** - The **{{ill|Great Temple of Abu Simbel|de|Tempel von Abu Simbel}}**, built by **{{ill|Ramesses II|de|Ramses II.}}** Amada relief of Thutmose III.jpg|{{ill|Thutmose III|de|Thutmosis III.}} in the temple of **Amada** before **Re-Harakhte** - 001 Torino Museo Egizio geroglifici su pietra.jpg|Cartouches of **Aten** (First didactic name of Aten) mentioning **Re-Harakhte** in the first **{{ill|cartouche (Ancient Egypt)|de|Kartusche (Altes Ägypten)}}** (read from right to left) Großer Tempel (Abu Simbel) 18.jpg|**Great Temple of Abu Simbel** Flickr - archer10 (Dennis) - Egypt-10C-024.jpg|**Great Temple of Abu Simbel**, sanctuary with **Re-Harakhte, Ramesses II, {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re}}, and {{ill|Ptah|de|Ptah}}** (from right to left) Maler der Grabkammer der Nefertari 001.jpg|**{{ill|Amentet|de|Amentet}}** in the form of **{{ill|Hathor|de|Hathor}}** with **Re-Harakhte** (Tomb of **{{ill|Nefertari (19th Dynasty)|de|Nefertari (19. Dynastie)}}**, **{{ill|QV66|de|QV66}}**) == Reception in the New Religious Movement Thelema == In **{{ill|Thelema|de|Thelema}}**, a **new religious movement founded by {{ill|Aleister Crowley|de|Aleister Crowley}}**, Re-Harakhte is known as **"Ra-Hoor-Khuit"** and represents **one of the three central principles of Thelemic doctrine.** "Ra-Hoor-Khuit" appears as the speaker of the **third chapter** of **{{ill|Liber AL vel Legis|de|Liber AL vel Legis}}**, the **sacred text of Thelema.**Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian deities|de|Liste ägyptischer Götter}} == References == * **Manfred Lurker**: ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Special edition, Scherz, Bern, 1998, ISBN 3-502-16430-4, p. 91. * **Rolf Felde**: ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' 2nd expanded and improved edition, R. Felde Publishing, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * **Hans Bonnet**: ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Religionsgeschichte.'' Nikol, Hamburg, 2000, ISBN 3-937872-08-6, pp. 269, 629–630. == External links == {{Commonscat|Ra-Horakhty|Re-Harakhte}} == Notes == [[Category:Egyptian deities]] [[Category:Solar deities]] Re-Harakhte was also worshiped in temples at: - **Wadi es-Sebua** - **{{ill|Amada (Nubian village)|de|Amada (nubisches Dorf)}}** - **{{ill|Temple of Derr|de|Tempel von Derr}}** - The **{{ill|Great Temple of Abu Simbel|de|Tempel von Abu Simbel}}**, built by **{{ill|Ramesses II|de|Ramses II.}}** Amada relief of Thutmose III.jpg|{{ill|Thutmose III|de|Thutmosis III.}} in the temple of **Amada** before **Re-Harakhte** - 001 Torino Museo Egizio geroglifici su pietra.jpg|Cartouches of **Aten** (First didactic name of Aten) mentioning **Re-Harakhte** in the first **{{ill|cartouche (Ancient Egypt)|de|Kartusche (Altes Ägypten)}}** (read from right to left) Großer Tempel (Abu Simbel) 18.jpg|**Great Temple of Abu Simbel** Flickr - archer10 (Dennis) - Egypt-10C-024.jpg|**Great Temple of Abu Simbel**, sanctuary with **Re-Harakhte, Ramesses II, {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re}}, and {{ill|Ptah|de|Ptah}}** (from right to left) Maler der Grabkammer der Nefertari 001.jpg|**{{ill|Amentet|de|Amentet}}** in the form of **{{ill|Hathor|de|Hathor}}** with **Re-Harakhte** (Tomb of **{{ill|Nefertari (19th Dynasty)|de|Nefertari (19. Dynastie)}}**, **{{ill|QV66|de|QV66}}**) == Reception in the New Religious Movement Thelema == In **{{ill|Thelema|de|Thelema}}**, a **new religious movement founded by {{ill|Aleister Crowley|de|Aleister Crowley}}**, Re-Harakhte is known as **"Ra-Hoor-Khuit"** and represents **one of the three central principles of Thelemic doctrine.** "Ra-Hoor-Khuit" appears as the speaker of the **third chapter** of **{{ill|Liber AL vel Legis|de|Liber AL vel Legis}}**, the **sacred text of Thelema.**Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian deities|de|Liste ägyptischer Götter}} == References == * **Manfred Lurker**: ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Special edition, Scherz, Bern, 1998, ISBN 3-502-16430-4, p. 91. * **Rolf Felde**: ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' 2nd expanded and improved edition, R. Felde Publishing, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * **Hans Bonnet**: ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Religionsgeschichte.'' Nikol, Hamburg, 2000, ISBN 3-937872-08-6, pp. 269, 629–630. == External links == {{Commonscat|Ra-Horakhty|Re-Harakhte}} == Notes == [[Category:Egyptian deities]] [[Category:Solar deities]] {{Infobox Egyptian Deity |IDEOGRAM = G9 |NAME-EXTENDED = Fully written out |NAME = ra:Z1-G7-Axt:t*y |NAME-EXPLANATION = Ra(u)-heru-achti
''Rˁ(w)-ḥr(.w)-3ḫ.tj''
''{{ill|Ra (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)}}-{{ill|Horus|de|Horus}}, Dweller of the Horizon'' |IMAGE1 = Stele Ra-Horakhty Louvre E5789.jpg |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 180px |IMAGE1-CAPTION = Priest Renpetmaa with {{ill|Unguent cone|de|Salbkegel}} before Re-Harakhte (Stele in the {{ill|Louvre|de|Louvre}}, ca. 900 BCE, {{ill|22nd Dynasty of Egypt|de|22. Dynastie}}) }} '''Re-Harakhte''' (also spelled '''Re-Horakhty''', '''Re-Hor-achti''') is a {{ill|solar deity|de|Sonnengott}} in {{ill|Egyptian mythology|de|Ägyptische Mythologie}}. This god emerged through the fusion ({{ill|Syncretism (Egyptology)|de|Synkretismus (Ägyptologie)}}) of {{ill|Ra (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)}} and {{ill|Harakhti|de|Harachte}}. == Depiction == Re-Harakhte is typically portrayed as a **standing or seated man** with **red skin** and a **falcon head**. On his head, he wears the **{{ill|solar disk|de|Sonnenscheibe}}**, encircled by the **{{ill|Uraeus serpent|de|Uräusschlange (Symbol)}}**. Other representations depict him as a **human with a ram's head** and a **solar disk**, such as in the **{{ill|Mortuary Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|de|Totentempel des Sethos I. (Abydos)}}**, or as having a **lion, cat, or falcon head with ram horns and a solar disk**, particularly in **({{ill|Hibis|de|Hibis}}).** == Meaning == Re-Harakhte describes Ra as the **rising sun in the east.** It is the actual name of the god **Ra of Heliopolis.**Rolf Felde: ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' Wiesbaden, 1995, p. 51. As the **Heliopolitan sun god**, he is highlighted as the **"ruler of the world."** In {{ill|Edfu|de|Edfu}}, **Re-Harakhte of Heliopolis** was regarded as **identical to the god {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}}.**M. Lurker: ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Bern, 1998, p. 91. {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao}} **{{ill|Akhenaten|de|Echnaton}}** called himself the **"High Priest of Re-Harakhte"**, and at the start of his reign, he depicted the god in his traditional form. However, in **the first so-called "{{ill|Didactic name of Aten|de|Aton#Der lehrhafte Name des Aton}}"** (Years 4–8 of his reign), he described **Aten** as **Re-Harakhte**: *"Long live the **Horizon Horus** (Re-Harakhte), who rejoices in the Light Land (Horizon)."* == Cult and Worship Sites == As early as **2445 BCE** ({{ill|5th Dynasty of Egypt|de|5. Dynastie}}), the **fusion form of these two gods** appeared in the **{{ill|Sun Temple of Nyuserre|de|Sonnenheiligtum des Niuserre}}** at **{{ill|Abusir|de|Abusir}}** (**{{ill|Abu Ghurab|de|Abu Ghurab}}**). Re-Harakhte was also worshiped in temples at: - **Wadi es-Sebua** - **{{ill|Amada (Nubian village)|de|Amada (nubisches Dorf)}}** - **{{ill|Temple of Derr|de|Tempel von Derr}}** - The **{{ill|Great Temple of Abu Simbel|de|Tempel von Abu Simbel}}**, built by **{{ill|Ramesses II|de|Ramses II.}}** Amada relief of Thutmose III.jpg|{{ill|Thutmose III|de|Thutmosis III.}} in the temple of **Amada** before **Re-Harakhte** - 001 Torino Museo Egizio geroglifici su pietra.jpg|Cartouches of **Aten** (First didactic name of Aten) mentioning **Re-Harakhte** in the first **{{ill|cartouche (Ancient Egypt)|de|Kartusche (Altes Ägypten)}}** (read from right to left) Großer Tempel (Abu Simbel) 18.jpg|**Great Temple of Abu Simbel** Flickr - archer10 (Dennis) - Egypt-10C-024.jpg|**Great Temple of Abu Simbel**, sanctuary with **Re-Harakhte, Ramesses II, {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re}}, and {{ill|Ptah|de|Ptah}}** (from right to left) Maler der Grabkammer der Nefertari 001.jpg|**{{ill|Amentet|de|Amentet}}** in the form of **{{ill|Hathor|de|Hathor}}** with **Re-Harakhte** (Tomb of **{{ill|Nefertari (19th Dynasty)|de|Nefertari (19. Dynastie)}}**, **{{ill|QV66|de|QV66}}**) == Reception in the New Religious Movement Thelema == In **{{ill|Thelema|de|Thelema}}**, a **new religious movement founded by {{ill|Aleister Crowley|de|Aleister Crowley}}**, Re-Harakhte is known as **"Ra-Hoor-Khuit"** and represents **one of the three central principles of Thelemic doctrine.** "Ra-Hoor-Khuit" appears as the speaker of the **third chapter** of **{{ill|Liber AL vel Legis|de|Liber AL vel Legis}}**, the **sacred text of Thelema.**Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian deities|de|Liste ägyptischer Götter}} == References == * **Manfred Lurker**: ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Special edition, Scherz, Bern, 1998, ISBN 3-502-16430-4, p. 91. * **Rolf Felde**: ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' 2nd expanded and improved edition, R. Felde Publishing, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * **Hans Bonnet**: ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Religionsgeschichte.'' Nikol, Hamburg, 2000, ISBN 3-937872-08-6, pp. 269, 629–630. == External links == {{Commonscat|Ra-Horakhty|Re-Harakhte}} == Notes == [[Category:Egyptian deities]] [[Category:Solar deities]]
