As the '''Heliopolitan sun god''', he is highlighted as the '''"ruler of the world."''' In {{ill|Edfu|de|Edfu}}, '''Re-Harakhte of Heliopolis''' was regarded as '''identical to the god {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}}.'''M. Lurker: ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Bern, 1998, p. 91. {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao}} '''{{ill|Akhenaten|de|Echnaton}}''' called himself the '''"High Priest of Re-Harakhte"''', and at the start of his reign, he depicted the god in his traditional form. However, in '''the first so-called "{{ill|Didactic name of Aten|de|Aton#Der lehrhafte Name des Aton}}"''' (Years 4–8 of his reign), he described '''Aten''' as '''Re-Harakhte''': *"Long live the '''Horizon Horus''' (Re-Harakhte), who rejoices in the Light Land (Horizon)."* == Cult and Worship Sites == As early as '''2445 BCE''' ({{ill|5th Dynasty of Egypt|de|5. Dynastie}}), the '''fusion form of these two gods''' appeared in the '''{{ill|Sun Temple of Nyuserre|de|Sonnenheiligtum des Niuserre}}''' at '''{{ill|Abusir|de|Abusir}}''' ('''{{ill|Abu Ghurab|de|Abu Ghurab}}'''). Re-Harakhte was also worshiped in temples at: - '''Wadi es-Sebua''' - '''{{ill|Amada (Nubian village)|de|Amada (nubisches Dorf)}}''' - '''{{ill|Temple of Derr|de|Tempel von Derr}}''' - The '''{{ill|Great Temple of Abu Simbel|de|Tempel von Abu Simbel}}''', built by '''{{ill|Ramesses II|de|Ramses II.}}''' Amada relief of Thutmose III.jpg|{{ill|Thutmose III|de|Thutmosis III.}} in the temple of '''Amada''' before '''Re-Harakhte''' - 001 Torino Museo Egizio geroglifici su pietra.jpg|Cartouches of '''Aten''' (First didactic name of Aten) mentioning '''Re-Harakhte''' in the first '''{{ill|cartouche (Ancient Egypt)|de|Kartusche (Altes Ägypten)}}''' (read from right to left) Großer Tempel (Abu Simbel) 18.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''' Flickr - archer10 (Dennis) - Egypt-10C-024.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''', sanctuary with '''Re-Harakhte, Ramesses II, {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re}}, and {{ill|Ptah|de|Ptah}}''' (from right to left) Maler der Grabkammer der Nefertari 001.jpg|'''{{ill|Amentet|de|Amentet}}''' in the form of '''{{ill|Hathor|de|Hathor}}''' with '''Re-Harakhte''' (Tomb of '''{{ill|Nefertari (19th Dynasty)|de|Nefertari (19. Dynastie)}}''', '''{{ill|QV66|de|QV66}}''') == Reception in the New Religious Movement Thelema == In '''{{ill|Thelema|de|Thelema}}''', a '''new religious movement founded by {{ill|Aleister Crowley|de|Aleister Crowley}}''', Re-Harakhte is known as '''"Ra-Hoor-Khuit"''' and represents '''one of the three central principles of Thelemic doctrine.''' "Ra-Hoor-Khuit" appears as the speaker of the '''third chapter''' of '''{{ill|Liber AL vel Legis|de|Liber AL vel Legis}}''', the '''sacred text of Thelema.'''Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian deities|de|Liste ägyptischer Götter}} == References == * '''Manfred Lurker''': ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Special edition, Scherz, Bern, 1998, ISBN 3-502-16430-4, p. 91. * '''Rolf Felde''': ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' 2nd expanded and improved edition, R. Felde Publishing, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * '''Hans Bonnet''': ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Religionsgeschichte.'' Nikol, Hamburg, 2000, ISBN 3-937872-08-6, pp. 269, 629–630. == External links == {{Commonscat|Ra-Horakhty|Re-Harakhte}} == Notes == [[Category:Egyptian deities]] [[Category:Solar deities]] {{Infobox Egyptian Deity |IDEOGRAM = G9 |NAME-EXTENDED = Fully written out |NAME = ra:Z1-G7-Axt:t*y |NAME-EXPLANATION = Ra(u)-heru-achti
''Rˁ(w)-ḥr(.w)-3ḫ.tj''
''{{ill|Ra (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)}}-{{ill|Horus|de|Horus}}, Dweller of the Horizon'' |IMAGE1 = Stele Ra-Horakhty Louvre E5789.jpg |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 180px |IMAGE1-CAPTION = Priest Renpetmaa with {{ill|Unguent cone|de|Salbkegel}} before Re-Harakhte (Stele in the {{ill|Louvre|de|Louvre}}, ca. 900 BCE, {{ill|22nd Dynasty of Egypt|de|22. Dynastie}}) }} '''Re-Harakhte''' (also spelled '''Re-Horakhty''', '''Re-Hor-achti''') is a {{ill|solar deity|de|Sonnengott}} in {{ill|Egyptian mythology|de|Ägyptische Mythologie}}. This god emerged through the fusion ({{ill|Syncretism (Egyptology)|de|Synkretismus (Ägyptologie)}}) of {{ill|Ra (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)}} and {{ill|Horakhty|de|Harachte}}. == Depiction == Re-Harakhte is typically portrayed as a '''standing or seated man''' with '''red skin''' and a '''falcon head'''. On his head, he wears the '''{{ill|solar disk|de|Sonnenscheibe}}''', encircled by the '''{{ill|Uraeus serpent|de|Uräusschlange (Symbol)}}'''. Other representations depict him as a '''human with a ram's head''' and a '''solar disk''', such as in the '''{{ill|Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|lt=Mortuary Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|de|Totentempel des Sethos I. (Abydos)}}''', or as having a '''lion, cat, or falcon head with ram horns and a solar disk''', particularly in '''({{ill|Hibis|de|Hibis}}).''' == Meaning == Re-Harakhte describes Ra as the '''rising sun in the east.''' It is the actual name of the god '''Ra of Heliopolis.'''Rolf Felde: ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' Wiesbaden, 1995, p. 51. As the '''Heliopolitan sun god''', he is highlighted as the '''"ruler of the world."''' In {{ill|Edfu|de|Edfu}}, '''Re-Harakhte of Heliopolis''' was regarded as '''identical to the god {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}}.'''M. Lurker: ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Bern, 1998, p. 91. {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao}} '''{{ill|Akhenaten|de|Echnaton}}''' called himself the '''"High Priest of Re-Harakhte"''', and at the start of his reign, he depicted the god in his traditional form. However, in '''the first so-called "{{ill|Aten|lt=Didactic name of Aten|de|Aton#Der lehrhafte Name des Aton}}"''' (Years 4–8 of his reign), he described '''Aten''' as '''Re-Harakhte''': *"Long live the '''Horizon Horus''' (Re-Harakhte), who rejoices in the Light Land (Horizon)."* == Cult and Worship Sites == As early as '''2445 BCE''' ({{ill|5th Dynasty of Egypt|de|5. Dynastie}}), the '''fusion form of these two gods''' appeared in the '''{{ill|Sun Temple of Nyuserre|de|Sonnenheiligtum des Niuserre}}''' at '''{{ill|Abusir|de|Abusir}}''' ('''{{ill|Abu Ghurab|de|Abu Ghurab}}'''). Re-Harakhte was also worshiped in temples at: - '''Wadi es-Sebua''' - '''{{ill|Amada (Nubian village)|de|Amada (nubisches Dorf)}}''' - '''{{ill|Temple of Derr|de|Tempel von Derr}}''' - The '''{{ill|Great Temple of Abu Simbel|de|Tempel von Abu Simbel}}''', built by '''{{ill|Ramesses II|de|Ramses II.}}''' Amada relief of Thutmose III.jpg|{{ill|Thutmose III|de|Thutmosis III.}} in the temple of '''Amada''' before '''Re-Harakhte''' - 001 Torino Museo Egizio geroglifici su pietra.jpg|Cartouches of '''Aten''' (First didactic name of Aten) mentioning '''Re-Harakhte''' in the first '''{{ill|cartouche (Ancient Egypt)|de|Kartusche (Altes Ägypten)}}''' (read from right to left) Großer Tempel (Abu Simbel) 18.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''' Flickr - archer10 (Dennis) - Egypt-10C-024.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''', sanctuary with '''Re-Harakhte, Ramesses II, {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re}}, and {{ill|Ptah|de|Ptah}}''' (from right to left) Maler der Grabkammer der Nefertari 001.jpg|'''{{ill|Amentet|de|Amentet}}''' in the form of '''{{ill|Hathor|de|Hathor}}''' with '''Re-Harakhte''' (Tomb of '''{{ill|Nefertari (19th Dynasty)|de|Nefertari (19. Dynastie)}}''', '''{{ill|QV66|de|QV66}}''') == Reception in the New Religious Movement Thelema == In '''{{ill|Thelema|de|Thelema}}''', a '''new religious movement founded by {{ill|Aleister Crowley|de|Aleister Crowley}}''', Re-Harakhte is known as '''"Ra-Hoor-Khuit"''' and represents '''one of the three central principles of Thelemic doctrine.''' "Ra-Hoor-Khuit" appears as the speaker of the '''third chapter''' of '''{{ill|Liber AL vel Legis|de|Liber AL vel Legis}}''', the '''sacred text of Thelema.'''Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian deities|de|Liste ägyptischer Götter}} == References == * '''Manfred Lurker''': ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Special edition, Scherz, Bern, 1998, ISBN 3-502-16430-4, p. 91. * '''Rolf Felde''': ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' 2nd expanded and improved edition, R. Felde Publishing, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * '''Hans Bonnet''': ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Religionsgeschichte.'' Nikol, Hamburg, 2000, ISBN 3-937872-08-6, pp. 269, 629–630. == External links == {{Commonscat|Ra-Horakhty|Re-Harakhte}} == Notes == [[Category:Egyptian deities]] [[Category:Solar deities]] Bern, 1998, p. 91. {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao}} '''{{ill|Akhenaten|de|Echnaton}}''' called himself the '''"High Priest of Re-Harakhte"''', and at the start of his reign, he depicted the god in his traditional form. Dynastie}}), the '''fusion form of these two gods''' appeared in the '''{{ill|Sun Temple of Nyuserre|de|Sonnenheiligtum des Niuserre}}''' at '''{{ill|Abusir|de|Abusir}}''' ('''{{ill|Abu Ghurab|de|Abu Ghurab}}'''). Re-Harakhte was also worshiped in temples at: - '''Wadi es-Sebua''' - '''{{ill|Amada (Nubian village)|de|Amada (nubisches Dorf)}}''' - '''{{ill|Temple of Derr|de|Tempel von Derr}}''' - The '''{{ill|Great Temple of Abu Simbel|de|Tempel von Abu Simbel}}''', built by '''{{ill|Ramesses II|de|Ramses II.}}''' Amada relief of Thutmose III.jpg|{{ill|Thutmose III|de|Thutmosis III.}} in the temple of '''Amada''' before '''Re-Harakhte''' - 001 Torino Museo Egizio geroglifici su pietra.jpg|Cartouches of '''Aten''' (First didactic name of Aten) mentioning '''Re-Harakhte''' in the first '''{{ill|cartouche (Ancient Egypt)|de|Kartusche (Altes Ägypten)}}''' (read from right to left) Großer Tempel (Abu Simbel) 18.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''' Flickr - archer10 (Dennis) - Egypt-10C-024.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''', sanctuary with '''Re-Harakhte, Ramesses II, {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re}}, and {{ill|Ptah|de|Ptah}}''' (from right to left) Maler der Grabkammer der Nefertari 001.jpg|'''{{ill|Amentet|de|Amentet}}''' in the form of '''{{ill|Hathor|de|Hathor}}''' with '''Re-Harakhte''' (Tomb of '''{{ill|Nefertari (19th Dynasty)|de|Nefertari (19. Dynastie)}}''', '''{{ill|QV66|de|QV66}}''') == Reception in the New Religious Movement Thelema == In '''{{ill|Thelema|de|Thelema}}''', a '''new religious movement founded by {{ill|Aleister Crowley|de|Aleister Crowley}}''', Re-Harakhte is known as '''"Ra-Hoor-Khuit"''' and represents '''one of the three central principles of Thelemic doctrine.''' {{Infobox Egyptian Deity |IDEOGRAM = G9 |NAME-EXTENDED = Fully written out |NAME = ra:Z1-G7-Axt:t*y |NAME-EXPLANATION = Ra(u)-heru-achti
''Rˁ(w)-ḥr(.w)-3ḫ.tj''
''{{ill|Ra (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)}}-{{ill|Horus|de|Horus}}, Dweller of the Horizon'' |IMAGE1 = Stele Ra-Horakhty Louvre E5789.jpg |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 180px |IMAGE1-CAPTION = Priest Renpetmaa with {{ill|Unguent cone|de|Salbkegel}} before Re-Harakhte (Stele in the {{ill|Louvre|de|Louvre}}, ca. 900 BCE, {{ill|22nd Dynasty of Egypt|de|22. Dynastie}}) }} '''Re-Harakhte''' (also spelled '''Re-Horakhty''', '''Re-Hor-achti''') is a {{ill|solar deity|de|Sonnengott}} in {{ill|Egyptian mythology|de|Ägyptische Mythologie}}. This god emerged through the fusion ({{ill|Syncretism (Egyptology)|de|Synkretismus (Ägyptologie)}}) of {{ill|Ra (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)}} and {{ill|Horakhty|de|Harachte}}. == Depiction == Re-Harakhte is typically portrayed as a '''standing or seated man''' with '''red skin''' and a '''falcon head'''. On his head, he wears the '''{{ill|solar disk|de|Sonnenscheibe}}''', encircled by the '''{{ill|Uraeus serpent|de|Uräusschlange (Symbol)}}'''. Other representations depict him as a '''human with a ram's head''' and a '''solar disk''', such as in the '''{{ill|Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|lt=Mortuary Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|de|Totentempel des Sethos I. (Abydos)}}''', or as having a '''lion, cat, or falcon head with ram horns and a solar disk''', particularly in '''({{ill|Hibis|de|Hibis}}).''' == Meaning == Re-Harakhte describes Ra as the '''rising sun in the east.''' It is the actual name of the god '''Ra of Heliopolis.'''Rolf Felde: ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' Wiesbaden, 1995, p. 51. As the '''Heliopolitan sun god''', he is highlighted as the '''"ruler of the world."''' In {{ill|Edfu|de|Edfu}}, '''Re-Harakhte of Heliopolis''' was regarded as '''identical to the god {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}}.'''M. Lurker: ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Bern, 1998, p. 91. {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao}} '''{{ill|Akhenaten|de|Echnaton}}''' called himself the '''"High Priest of Re-Harakhte"''', and at the start of his reign, he depicted the god in his traditional form. However, in '''the first so-called "{{ill|Aten|lt=Didactic name of Aten|de|Aton#Der lehrhafte Name des Aton}}"''' (Years 4–8 of his reign), he described '''Aten''' as '''Re-Harakhte''': *"Long live the '''Horizon Horus''' (Re-Harakhte), who rejoices in the Light Land (Horizon)."* == Cult and Worship Sites == As early as '''2445 BCE''' ({{ill|5th Dynasty of Egypt|de|5. Dynastie}}), the '''fusion form of these two gods''' appeared in the '''{{ill|Sun Temple of Nyuserre|de|Sonnenheiligtum des Niuserre}}''' at '''{{ill|Abusir|de|Abusir}}''' ('''{{ill|Abu Ghurab|de|Abu Ghurab}}'''). Re-Harakhte was also worshiped in temples at: - '''Wadi es-Sebua''' - '''{{ill|Amada (Nubian village)|de|Amada (nubisches Dorf)}}''' - '''{{ill|Temple of Derr|de|Tempel von Derr}}''' - The '''{{ill|Great Temple of Abu Simbel|de|Tempel von Abu Simbel}}''', built by '''{{ill|Ramesses II|de|Ramses II.}}''' Amada relief of Thutmose III.jpg|{{ill|Thutmose III|de|Thutmosis III.}} in the temple of '''Amada''' before '''Re-Harakhte''' - 001 Torino Museo Egizio geroglifici su pietra.jpg|Cartouches of '''Aten''' (First didactic name of Aten) mentioning '''Re-Harakhte''' in the first '''{{ill|cartouche (Ancient Egypt)|de|Kartusche (Altes Ägypten)}}''' (read from right to left) Großer Tempel (Abu Simbel) 18.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''' Flickr - archer10 (Dennis) - Egypt-10C-024.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''', sanctuary with '''Re-Harakhte, Ramesses II, {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re}}, and {{ill|Ptah|de|Ptah}}''' (from right to left) Maler der Grabkammer der Nefertari 001.jpg|'''{{ill|Amentet|de|Amentet}}''' in the form of '''{{ill|Hathor|de|Hathor}}''' with '''Re-Harakhte''' (Tomb of '''{{ill|Nefertari (19th Dynasty)|de|Nefertari (19. Dynastie)}}''', '''{{ill|QV66|de|QV66}}''') == Reception in the New Religious Movement Thelema == In '''{{ill|Thelema|de|Thelema}}''', a '''new religious movement founded by {{ill|Aleister Crowley|de|Aleister Crowley}}''', Re-Harakhte is known as '''"Ra-Hoor-Khuit"''' and represents '''one of the three central principles of Thelemic doctrine.''' "Ra-Hoor-Khuit" appears as the speaker of the '''third chapter''' of '''{{ill|Liber AL vel Legis|de|Liber AL vel Legis}}''', the '''sacred text of Thelema.'''Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian deities|de|Liste ägyptischer Götter}} == References == * '''Manfred Lurker''': ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Special edition, Scherz, Bern, 1998, ISBN 3-502-16430-4, p. 91. * '''Rolf Felde''': ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' 2nd expanded and improved edition, R. Felde Publishing, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * '''Hans Bonnet''': ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Religionsgeschichte.'' Nikol, Hamburg, 2000, ISBN 3-937872-08-6, pp. 269, 629–630. == External links == {{Commonscat|Ra-Horakhty|Re-Harakhte}} == Notes == [[Category:Egyptian deities]] [[Category:Solar deities]] {{Infobox Egyptian Deity |IDEOGRAM = G9 |NAME-EXTENDED = Fully written out |NAME = ra:Z1-G7-Axt:t*y |NAME-EXPLANATION = Ra(u)-heru-achti
''Rˁ(w)-ḥr(.w)-3ḫ.tj''
''{{ill|Ra (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)}}-{{ill|Horus|de|Horus}}, Dweller of the Horizon'' |IMAGE1 = Stele Ra-Horakhty Louvre E5789.jpg |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 180px |IMAGE1-CAPTION = Priest Renpetmaa with {{ill|Unguent cone|de|Salbkegel}} before Re-Harakhte (Stele in the {{ill|Louvre|de|Louvre}}, ca. 900 BCE, {{ill|22nd Dynasty of Egypt|de|22. Dynastie}}) }} '''Re-Harakhte''' (also spelled '''Re-Horakhty''', '''Re-Hor-achti''') is a {{ill|solar deity|de|Sonnengott}} in {{ill|Egyptian mythology|de|Ägyptische Mythologie}}. This god emerged through the fusion ({{ill|Syncretism (Egyptology)|de|Synkretismus (Ägyptologie)}}) of {{ill|Ra (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)}} and {{ill|Horakhty|de|Harachte}}. == Depiction == Re-Harakhte is typically portrayed as a '''standing or seated man''' with '''red skin''' and a '''falcon head'''. On his head, he wears the '''{{ill|solar disk|de|Sonnenscheibe}}''', encircled by the '''{{ill|Uraeus serpent|de|Uräusschlange (Symbol)}}'''. Other representations depict him as a '''human with a ram's head''' and a '''solar disk''', such as in the '''{{ill|Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|lt=Mortuary Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|de|Totentempel des Sethos I. (Abydos)}}''', or as having a '''lion, cat, or falcon head with ram horns and a solar disk''', particularly in '''({{ill|Hibis|de|Hibis}}).''' == Meaning == Re-Harakhte describes Ra as the '''rising sun in the east.''' It is the actual name of the god '''Ra of Heliopolis.'''Rolf Felde: ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' Wiesbaden, 1995, p. 51. As the '''Heliopolitan sun god''', he is highlighted as the '''"ruler of the world."''' In {{ill|Edfu|de|Edfu}}, '''Re-Harakhte of Heliopolis''' was regarded as '''identical to the god {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}}.'''M. Lurker: ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Bern, 1998, p. 91. {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao}} '''{{ill|Akhenaten|de|Echnaton}}''' called himself the '''"High Priest of Re-Harakhte"''', and at the start of his reign, he depicted the god in his traditional form. However, in '''the first so-called "{{ill|Aten|lt=Didactic name of Aten|de|Aton#Der lehrhafte Name des Aton}}"''' (Years 4–8 of his reign), he described '''Aten''' as '''Re-Harakhte''': *"Long live the '''Horizon Horus''' (Re-Harakhte), who rejoices in the Light Land (Horizon)."* == Cult and Worship Sites == As early as '''2445 BCE''' ({{ill|5th Dynasty of Egypt|de|5. Dynastie}}), the '''fusion form of these two gods''' appeared in the '''{{ill|Sun Temple of Nyuserre|de|Sonnenheiligtum des Niuserre}}''' at '''{{ill|Abusir|de|Abusir}}''' ('''{{ill|Abu Ghurab|de|Abu Ghurab}}'''). Re-Harakhte was also worshiped in temples at: - '''Wadi es-Sebua''' - '''{{ill|Amada (Nubian village)|de|Amada (nubisches Dorf)}}''' - '''{{ill|Temple of Derr|de|Tempel von Derr}}''' - The '''{{ill|Great Temple of Abu Simbel|de|Tempel von Abu Simbel}}''', built by '''{{ill|Ramesses II|de|Ramses II.}}''' Amada relief of Thutmose III.jpg|{{ill|Thutmose III|de|Thutmosis III.}} in the temple of '''Amada''' before '''Re-Harakhte''' - 001 Torino Museo Egizio geroglifici su pietra.jpg|Cartouches of '''Aten''' (First didactic name of Aten) mentioning '''Re-Harakhte''' in the first '''{{ill|cartouche (Ancient Egypt)|de|Kartusche (Altes Ägypten)}}''' (read from right to left) Großer Tempel (Abu Simbel) 18.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''' Flickr - archer10 (Dennis) - Egypt-10C-024.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''', sanctuary with '''Re-Harakhte, Ramesses II, {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re}}, and {{ill|Ptah|de|Ptah}}''' (from right to left) Maler der Grabkammer der Nefertari 001.jpg|'''{{ill|Amentet|de|Amentet}}''' in the form of '''{{ill|Hathor|de|Hathor}}''' with '''Re-Harakhte''' (Tomb of '''{{ill|Nefertari (19th Dynasty)|de|Nefertari (19. Dynastie)}}''', '''{{ill|QV66|de|QV66}}''') == Reception in the New Religious Movement Thelema == In '''{{ill|Thelema|de|Thelema}}''', a '''new religious movement founded by {{ill|Aleister Crowley|de|Aleister Crowley}}''', Re-Harakhte is known as '''"Ra-Hoor-Khuit"''' and represents '''one of the three central principles of Thelemic doctrine.''' "Ra-Hoor-Khuit" appears as the speaker of the '''third chapter''' of '''{{ill|Liber AL vel Legis|de|Liber AL vel Legis}}''', the '''sacred text of Thelema.'''Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian deities|de|Liste ägyptischer Götter}} == References == * '''Manfred Lurker''': ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Special edition, Scherz, Bern, 1998, ISBN 3-502-16430-4, p. 91. * '''Rolf Felde''': ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' 2nd expanded and improved edition, R. Felde Publishing, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * '''Hans Bonnet''': ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Religionsgeschichte.'' Nikol, Hamburg, 2000, ISBN 3-937872-08-6, pp. 269, 629–630. == External links == {{Commonscat|Ra-Horakhty|Re-Harakhte}} == Notes == [[Category:Egyptian deities]] [[Category:Solar deities]] {{Infobox Egyptian Deity |IDEOGRAM = G9 |NAME-EXTENDED = Fully written out |NAME = ra:Z1-G7-Axt:t*y |NAME-EXPLANATION = Ra(u)-heru-achti
''Rˁ(w)-ḥr(.w)-3ḫ.tj''
''{{ill|Ra (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)}}-{{ill|Horus|de|Horus}}, Dweller of the Horizon'' |IMAGE1 = Stele Ra-Horakhty Louvre E5789.jpg |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 180px |IMAGE1-CAPTION = Priest Renpetmaa with {{ill|Unguent cone|de|Salbkegel}} before Re-Harakhte (Stele in the {{ill|Louvre|de|Louvre}}, ca. 900 BCE, {{ill|22nd Dynasty of Egypt|de|22. Dynastie}}) }} '''Re-Harakhte''' (also spelled '''Re-Horakhty''', '''Re-Hor-achti''') is a {{ill|solar deity|de|Sonnengott}} in {{ill|Egyptian mythology|de|Ägyptische Mythologie}}. This god emerged through the fusion ({{ill|Syncretism (Egyptology)|de|Synkretismus (Ägyptologie)}}) of {{ill|Ra (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)}} and {{ill|Horakhty|de|Harachte}}. == Depiction == Re-Harakhte is typically portrayed as a '''standing or seated man''' with '''red skin''' and a '''falcon head'''. On his head, he wears the '''{{ill|solar disk|de|Sonnenscheibe}}''', encircled by the '''{{ill|Uraeus serpent|de|Uräusschlange (Symbol)}}'''. Other representations depict him as a '''human with a ram's head''' and a '''solar disk''', such as in the '''{{ill|Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|lt=Mortuary Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|de|Totentempel des Sethos I. (Abydos)}}''', or as having a '''lion, cat, or falcon head with ram horns and a solar disk''', particularly in '''({{ill|Hibis|de|Hibis}}).''' == Meaning == Re-Harakhte describes Ra as the '''rising sun in the east.''' It is the actual name of the god '''Ra of Heliopolis.'''Rolf Felde: ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' Wiesbaden, 1995, p. 51. As the '''Heliopolitan sun god''', he is highlighted as the '''"ruler of the world."''' In {{ill|Edfu|de|Edfu}}, '''Re-Harakhte of Heliopolis''' was regarded as '''identical to the god {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}}.'''M. Lurker: ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Bern, 1998, p. 91. {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao}} '''{{ill|Akhenaten|de|Echnaton}}''' called himself the '''"High Priest of Re-Harakhte"''', and at the start of his reign, he depicted the god in his traditional form. However, in '''the first so-called "{{ill|Aten|lt=Didactic name of Aten|de|Aton#Der lehrhafte Name des Aton}}"''' (Years 4–8 of his reign), he described '''Aten''' as '''Re-Harakhte''': *"Long live the '''Horizon Horus''' (Re-Harakhte), who rejoices in the Light Land (Horizon)."* == Cult and Worship Sites == As early as '''2445 BCE''' ({{ill|5th Dynasty of Egypt|de|5. Dynastie}}), the '''fusion form of these two gods''' appeared in the '''{{ill|Sun Temple of Nyuserre|de|Sonnenheiligtum des Niuserre}}''' at '''{{ill|Abusir|de|Abusir}}''' ('''{{ill|Abu Ghurab|de|Abu Ghurab}}'''). Re-Harakhte was also worshiped in temples at: - '''Wadi es-Sebua''' - '''{{ill|Amada (Nubian village)|de|Amada (nubisches Dorf)}}''' - '''{{ill|Temple of Derr|de|Tempel von Derr}}''' - The '''{{ill|Great Temple of Abu Simbel|de|Tempel von Abu Simbel}}''', built by '''{{ill|Ramesses II|de|Ramses II.}}''' Amada relief of Thutmose III.jpg|{{ill|Thutmose III|de|Thutmosis III.}} in the temple of '''Amada''' before '''Re-Harakhte''' - 001 Torino Museo Egizio geroglifici su pietra.jpg|Cartouches of '''Aten''' (First didactic name of Aten) mentioning '''Re-Harakhte''' in the first '''{{ill|cartouche (Ancient Egypt)|de|Kartusche (Altes Ägypten)}}''' (read from right to left) Großer Tempel (Abu Simbel) 18.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''' Flickr - archer10 (Dennis) - Egypt-10C-024.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''', sanctuary with '''Re-Harakhte, Ramesses II, {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re}}, and {{ill|Ptah|de|Ptah}}''' (from right to left) Maler der Grabkammer der Nefertari 001.jpg|'''{{ill|Amentet|de|Amentet}}''' in the form of '''{{ill|Hathor|de|Hathor}}''' with '''Re-Harakhte''' (Tomb of '''{{ill|Nefertari (19th Dynasty)|de|Nefertari (19. Dynastie)}}''', '''{{ill|QV66|de|QV66}}''') == Reception in the New Religious Movement Thelema == In '''{{ill|Thelema|de|Thelema}}''', a '''new religious movement founded by {{ill|Aleister Crowley|de|Aleister Crowley}}''', Re-Harakhte is known as '''"Ra-Hoor-Khuit"''' and represents '''one of the three central principles of Thelemic doctrine.''' "Ra-Hoor-Khuit" appears as the speaker of the '''third chapter''' of '''{{ill|Liber AL vel Legis|de|Liber AL vel Legis}}''', the '''sacred text of Thelema.'''Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian deities|de|Liste ägyptischer Götter}} == References == * '''Manfred Lurker''': ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Special edition, Scherz, Bern, 1998, ISBN 3-502-16430-4, p. 91. * '''Rolf Felde''': ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' 2nd expanded and improved edition, R. Felde Publishing, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * '''Hans Bonnet''': ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Religionsgeschichte.'' Nikol, Hamburg, 2000, ISBN 3-937872-08-6, pp. 269, 629–630. == External links == {{Commonscat|Ra-Horakhty|Re-Harakhte}} == Notes == [[Category:Egyptian deities]] [[Category:Solar deities]] Felde Publishing, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * '''Hans Bonnet''': ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Religionsgeschichte.'' revision.comment: Text replacement - "Worship Site" to "{{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=Worship Site}}"
revision.text:
{{Infobox Egyptian Deity |IDEOGRAM = G9 |NAME-EXTENDED = Fully written out |NAME = ra:Z1-G7-Axt:t*y |NAME-EXPLANATION = Ra(u)-heru-achti
''Rˁ(w)-ḥr(.w)-3ḫ.tj''
''{{ill|Ra (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)}}-{{ill|Horus|de|Horus}}, Dweller of the Horizon'' |IMAGE1 = Stele Ra-Horakhty Louvre E5789.jpg |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 180px |IMAGE1-CAPTION = Priest Renpetmaa with {{ill|Unguent cone|de|Salbkegel}} before Re-Harakhte (Stele in the {{ill|Louvre|de|Louvre}}, ca. 900 BCE, {{ill|22nd Dynasty of Egypt|de|22. Dynastie}}) }} '''Re-Harakhte''' (also spelled '''Re-Horakhty''', '''Re-Hor-achti''') is a {{ill|solar deity|de|Sonnengott}} in {{ill|Egyptian mythology|de|Ägyptische Mythologie}}. This god emerged through the fusion ({{ill|Syncretism (Egyptology)|de|Synkretismus (Ägyptologie)}}) of {{ill|Ra (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)}} and {{ill|Horakhty|de|Harachte}}. == Depiction == Re-Harakhte is typically portrayed as a '''standing or seated man''' with '''red skin''' and a '''falcon head'''. On his head, he wears the '''{{ill|solar disk|de|Sonnenscheibe}}''', encircled by the '''{{ill|Uraeus serpent|de|Uräusschlange (Symbol)}}'''. Other representations depict him as a '''human with a ram's head''' and a '''solar disk''', such as in the '''{{ill|Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|lt=Mortuary Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|de|Totentempel des Sethos I. (Abydos)}}''', or as having a '''lion, cat, or falcon head with ram horns and a solar disk''', particularly in '''({{ill|Hibis|de|Hibis}}).''' == Meaning == Re-Harakhte describes Ra as the '''rising sun in the east.''' It is the actual name of the god '''Ra of Heliopolis.'''Rolf Felde: ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' Wiesbaden, 1995, p. 51. As the '''Heliopolitan sun god''', he is highlighted as the '''"ruler of the world."''' In {{ill|Edfu|de|Edfu}}, '''Re-Harakhte of Heliopolis''' was regarded as '''identical to the god {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}}.'''M. Lurker: ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Bern, 1998, p. 91. {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao}} '''{{ill|Akhenaten|de|Echnaton}}''' called himself the '''"High Priest of Re-Harakhte"''', and at the start of his reign, he depicted the god in his traditional form. However, in '''the first so-called "{{ill|Aten|lt=Didactic name of Aten|de|Aton#Der lehrhafte Name des Aton}}"''' (Years 4–8 of his reign), he described '''Aten''' as '''Re-Harakhte''': *"Long live the '''Horizon Horus''' (Re-Harakhte), who rejoices in the Light Land (Horizon)."* == Cult and {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=Worship Site}}s == As early as '''2445 BCE''' ({{ill|5th Dynasty of Egypt|de|5. Dynastie}}), the '''fusion form of these two gods''' appeared in the '''{{ill|Sun Temple of Nyuserre|de|Sonnenheiligtum des Niuserre}}''' at '''{{ill|Abusir|de|Abusir}}''' ('''{{ill|Abu Ghurab|de|Abu Ghurab}}'''). Re-Harakhte was also worshiped in temples at: - '''Wadi es-Sebua''' - '''{{ill|Amada (Nubian village)|de|Amada (nubisches Dorf)}}''' - '''{{ill|Temple of Derr|de|Tempel von Derr}}''' - The '''{{ill|Great Temple of Abu Simbel|de|Tempel von Abu Simbel}}''', built by '''{{ill|Ramesses II|de|Ramses II.}}''' Amada relief of Thutmose III.jpg|{{ill|Thutmose III|de|Thutmosis III.}} in the temple of '''Amada''' before '''Re-Harakhte''' - 001 Torino Museo Egizio geroglifici su pietra.jpg|Cartouches of '''Aten''' (First didactic name of Aten) mentioning '''Re-Harakhte''' in the first '''{{ill|cartouche (Ancient Egypt)|de|Kartusche (Altes Ägypten)}}''' (read from right to left) Großer Tempel (Abu Simbel) 18.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''' Flickr - archer10 (Dennis) - Egypt-10C-024.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''', sanctuary with '''Re-Harakhte, Ramesses II, {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re}}, and {{ill|Ptah|de|Ptah}}''' (from right to left) Maler der Grabkammer der Nefertari 001.jpg|'''{{ill|Amentet|de|Amentet}}''' in the form of '''{{ill|Hathor|de|Hathor}}''' with '''Re-Harakhte''' (Tomb of '''{{ill|Nefertari (19th Dynasty)|de|Nefertari (19. Dynastie)}}''', '''{{ill|QV66|de|QV66}}''') == Reception in the New Religious Movement Thelema == In '''{{ill|Thelema|de|Thelema}}''', a '''new religious movement founded by {{ill|Aleister Crowley|de|Aleister Crowley}}''', Re-Harakhte is known as '''"Ra-Hoor-Khuit"''' and represents '''one of the three central principles of Thelemic doctrine.''' "Ra-Hoor-Khuit" appears as the speaker of the '''third chapter''' of '''{{ill|Liber AL vel Legis|de|Liber AL vel Legis}}''', the '''sacred text of Thelema.'''Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian deities|de|Liste ägyptischer Götter}} == References == * '''Manfred Lurker''': ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Special edition, Scherz, Bern, 1998, ISBN 3-502-16430-4, p. 91. * '''Rolf Felde''': ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' 2nd expanded and improved edition, R. Felde Publishing, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * '''Hans Bonnet''': ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Religionsgeschichte.'' Nikol, Hamburg, 2000, ISBN 3-937872-08-6, pp. 269, 629–630. == External links == {{Commonscat|Ra-Horakhty|Re-Harakhte}} == Notes == [[Category:Egyptian deities]] [[Category:Solar deities]] {{Infobox Egyptian Deity |IDEOGRAM = G9 |NAME-EXTENDED = Fully written out |NAME = ra:Z1-G7-Axt:t*y |NAME-EXPLANATION = Ra(u)-heru-achti
''Rˁ(w)-ḥr(.w)-3ḫ.tj''
''{{ill|Ra (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)}}-{{ill|Horus|de|Horus}}, Dweller of the Horizon'' |IMAGE1 = Stele Ra-Horakhty Louvre E5789.jpg |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 180px |IMAGE1-CAPTION = Priest Renpetmaa with {{ill|Unguent cone|de|Salbkegel}} before Re-Harakhte (Stele in the {{ill|Louvre|de|Louvre}}, ca. 900 BCE, {{ill|22nd Dynasty of Egypt|de|22. Dynastie}}) }} '''Re-Harakhte''' (also spelled '''Re-Horakhty''', '''Re-Hor-achti''') is a {{ill|solar deity|de|Sonnengott}} in {{ill|Egyptian mythology|de|Ägyptische Mythologie}}. This god emerged through the fusion ({{ill|Syncretism (Egyptology)|de|Synkretismus (Ägyptologie)}}) of {{ill|Ra (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)}} and {{ill|Horakhty|de|Harachte}}. == Depiction == Re-Harakhte is typically portrayed as a '''standing or seated man''' with '''red skin''' and a '''falcon head'''. On his head, he wears the '''{{ill|solar disk|de|Sonnenscheibe}}''', encircled by the '''{{ill|Uraeus serpent|de|Uräusschlange (Symbol)}}'''. Other representations depict him as a '''human with a ram's head''' and a '''solar disk''', such as in the '''{{ill|Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|lt=Mortuary Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|de|Totentempel des Sethos I. (Abydos)}}''', or as having a '''lion, cat, or falcon head with ram horns and a solar disk''', particularly in '''({{ill|Hibis|de|Hibis}}).''' == Meaning == Re-Harakhte describes Ra as the '''rising sun in the east.''' It is the actual name of the god '''Ra of Heliopolis.'''Rolf Felde: ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' Wiesbaden, 1995, p. 51. As the '''Heliopolitan sun god''', he is highlighted as the '''"ruler of the world."''' In {{ill|Edfu|de|Edfu}}, '''Re-Harakhte of Heliopolis''' was regarded as '''identical to the god {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}}.'''M. Lurker: ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Bern, 1998, p. 91. {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao}} '''{{ill|Akhenaten|de|Echnaton}}''' called himself the '''"High Priest of Re-Harakhte"''', and at the start of his reign, he depicted the god in his traditional form. However, in '''the first so-called "{{ill|Aten|lt=Didactic name of Aten|de|Aton#Der lehrhafte Name des Aton}}"''' (Years 4–8 of his reign), he described '''Aten''' as '''Re-Harakhte''': *"Long live the '''Horizon Horus''' (Re-Harakhte), who rejoices in the Light Land (Horizon)."* == Cult and {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=Worship Sites}} == As early as '''2445 BCE''' ({{ill|5th Dynasty of Egypt|de|5. Dynastie}}), the '''fusion form of these two gods''' appeared in the '''{{ill|Sun Temple of Nyuserre|de|Sonnenheiligtum des Niuserre}}''' at '''{{ill|Abusir|de|Abusir}}''' ('''{{ill|Abu Ghurab|de|Abu Ghurab}}'''). Re-Harakhte was also worshiped in temples at: - '''Wadi es-Sebua''' - '''{{ill|Amada (Nubian village)|de|Amada (nubisches Dorf)}}''' - '''{{ill|Temple of Derr|de|Tempel von Derr}}''' - The '''{{ill|Great Temple of Abu Simbel|de|Tempel von Abu Simbel}}''', built by '''{{ill|Ramesses II|de|Ramses II.}}''' Amada relief of Thutmose III.jpg|{{ill|Thutmose III|de|Thutmosis III.}} in the temple of '''Amada''' before '''Re-Harakhte''' - 001 Torino Museo Egizio geroglifici su pietra.jpg|Cartouches of '''Aten''' (First didactic name of Aten) mentioning '''Re-Harakhte''' in the first '''{{ill|cartouche (Ancient Egypt)|de|Kartusche (Altes Ägypten)}}''' (read from right to left) Großer Tempel (Abu Simbel) 18.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''' Flickr - archer10 (Dennis) - Egypt-10C-024.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''', sanctuary with '''Re-Harakhte, Ramesses II, {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re}}, and {{ill|Ptah|de|Ptah}}''' (from right to left) Maler der Grabkammer der Nefertari 001.jpg|'''{{ill|Amentet|de|Amentet}}''' in the form of '''{{ill|Hathor|de|Hathor}}''' with '''Re-Harakhte''' (Tomb of '''{{ill|Nefertari (19th Dynasty)|de|Nefertari (19. Dynastie)}}''', '''{{ill|QV66|de|QV66}}''') == Reception in the New Religious Movement Thelema == In '''{{ill|Thelema|de|Thelema}}''', a '''new religious movement founded by {{ill|Aleister Crowley|de|Aleister Crowley}}''', Re-Harakhte is known as '''"Ra-Hoor-Khuit"''' and represents '''one of the three central principles of Thelemic doctrine.''' "Ra-Hoor-Khuit" appears as the speaker of the '''third chapter''' of '''{{ill|Liber AL vel Legis|de|Liber AL vel Legis}}''', the '''sacred text of Thelema.'''Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian deities|de|Liste ägyptischer Götter}} == References == * '''Manfred Lurker''': ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Special edition, Scherz, Bern, 1998, ISBN 3-502-16430-4, p. 91. * '''Rolf Felde''': ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' 2nd expanded and improved edition, R. Felde Publishing, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * '''Hans Bonnet''': ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Religionsgeschichte.'' Nikol, Hamburg, 2000, ISBN 3-937872-08-6, pp. 269, 629–630. == External links == {{Commonscat|Ra-Horakhty|Re-Harakhte}} == Notes == [[Category:Egyptian deities]] [[Category:Solar deities]] Re-Harakhte was also worshiped in temples at: - '''Wadi es-Sebua''' - '''{{ill|Amada (Nubian village)|de|Amada (nubisches Dorf)}}''' - '''{{ill|Temple of Derr|de|Tempel von Derr}}''' - The '''{{ill|Great Temple of Abu Simbel|de|Tempel von Abu Simbel}}''', built by '''{{ill|Ramesses II|de|Ramses II.}}''' Amada relief of Thutmose III.jpg|{{ill|Thutmose III|de|Thutmosis III.}} in the temple of '''Amada''' before '''Re-Harakhte''' - 001 Torino Museo Egizio geroglifici su pietra.jpg|Cartouches of '''Aten''' (First didactic name of Aten) mentioning '''Re-Harakhte''' in the first '''{{ill|cartouche (Ancient Egypt)|de|Kartusche (Altes Ägypten)}}''' (read from right to left) Großer Tempel (Abu Simbel) 18.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''' Flickr - archer10 (Dennis) - Egypt-10C-024.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''', sanctuary with '''Re-Harakhte, Ramesses II, {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re}}, and {{ill|Ptah|de|Ptah}}''' (from right to left) Maler der Grabkammer der Nefertari 001.jpg|'''{{ill|Amentet|de|Amentet}}''' in the form of '''{{ill|Hathor|de|Hathor}}''' with '''Re-Harakhte''' (Tomb of '''{{ill|Nefertari (19th Dynasty)|de|Nefertari (19. Dynastie)}}''', '''{{ill|QV66|de|QV66}}''') == Reception in the New Religious Movement Thelema == In '''{{ill|Thelema|de|Thelema}}''', a '''new religious movement founded by {{ill|Aleister Crowley|de|Aleister Crowley}}''', Re-Harakhte is known as '''"Ra-Hoor-Khuit"''' and represents '''one of the three central principles of Thelemic doctrine.''' "Ra-Hoor-Khuit" appears as the speaker of the '''third chapter''' of '''{{ill|Liber AL vel Legis|de|Liber AL vel Legis}}''', the '''sacred text of Thelema.'''Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian deities|de|Liste ägyptischer Götter}} == References == * '''Manfred Lurker''': ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Felde Publishing, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * '''Hans Bonnet''': ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Religionsgeschichte.'' Nikol, Hamburg, 2000, ISBN 3-937872-08-6, pp. 269, 629–630. == External links == {{Commonscat|Ra-Horakhty|Re-Harakhte}} == Notes == [[Category:Egyptian deities]] [[Category:Solar deities]] {{Infobox Egyptian Deity |IDEOGRAM = G9 |NAME-EXTENDED = Fully written out |NAME = ra:Z1-G7-Axt:t*y |NAME-EXPLANATION = Ra(u)-heru-achti
''Rˁ(w)-ḥr(.w)-3ḫ.tj''
''{{ill|Ra (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)}}-{{ill|Horus|de|Horus}}, Dweller of the Horizon'' |IMAGE1 = Stele Ra-Horakhty Louvre E5789.jpg |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 180px |IMAGE1-CAPTION = Priest Renpetmaa with {{ill|Unguent cone|de|Salbkegel}} before Re-Harakhte (Stele in the {{ill|Louvre|de|Louvre}}, ca. 900 BCE, {{ill|22nd Dynasty of Egypt|de|22. Dynastie}}) }} '''Re-Harakhte''' (also spelled '''Re-Horakhty''', '''Re-Hor-achti''') is a {{ill|solar deity|de|Sonnengott}} in {{ill|Egyptian mythology|de|Ägyptische Mythologie}}. This god emerged through the fusion ({{ill|Syncretism (Egyptology)|de|Synkretismus (Ägyptologie)}}) of {{ill|Ra (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)}} and {{ill|Horakhty|de|Harachte}}. == Depiction == Re-Harakhte is typically portrayed as a '''standing or seated man''' with '''red skin''' and a '''falcon head'''. On his head, he wears the '''{{ill|solar disk|de|Sonnenscheibe}}''', encircled by the '''{{ill|Uraeus serpent|de|Uräusschlange (Symbol)}}'''. Other representations depict him as a '''human with a ram's head''' and a '''solar disk''', such as in the '''{{ill|Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|lt=Mortuary Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|de|Totentempel des Sethos I. (Abydos)}}''', or as having a '''lion, cat, or falcon head with ram horns and a solar disk''', particularly in '''({{ill|Hibis|de|Hibis}}).''' == Meaning == Re-Harakhte describes Ra as the '''rising sun in the east.''' It is the actual name of the god '''Ra of Heliopolis.'''Rolf Felde: ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' Wiesbaden, 1995, p. 51. As the '''Heliopolitan sun god''', he is highlighted as the '''"ruler of the world."''' In {{ill|Edfu|de|Edfu}}, '''Re-Harakhte of Heliopolis''' was regarded as '''identical to the god {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}}.'''M. Lurker: ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Bern, 1998, p. 91. {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao}} '''{{ill|Akhenaten|de|Echnaton}}''' called himself the '''"High Priest of Re-Harakhte"''', and at the start of his reign, he depicted the god in his traditional form. However, in '''the first so-called "{{ill|Aten|lt=Didactic name of Aten|de|Aton#Der lehrhafte Name des Aton}}"''' (Years 4–8 of his reign), he described '''Aten''' as '''Re-Harakhte''': *"Long live the '''Horizon Horus''' (Re-Harakhte), who rejoices in the Light Land (Horizon)."* == Cult and {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=Worship Sites}} == As early as '''2445 BCE''' ({{ill|5th Dynasty of Egypt|de|5. Dynastie}}), the '''fusion form of these two gods''' appeared in the '''{{ill|Sun Temple of Nyuserre|de|Sonnenheiligtum des Niuserre}}''' at '''{{ill|Abusir|de|Abusir}}''' ('''{{ill|Abu Ghurab|de|Abu Ghurab}}'''). Re-Harakhte was also worshiped in temples at: - '''Wadi es-Sebua''' - '''{{ill|Amada (Nubian village)|de|Amada (nubisches Dorf)}}''' - '''{{ill|Temple of Derr|de|Tempel von Derr}}''' - The '''{{ill|Great Temple of Abu Simbel|de|Tempel von Abu Simbel}}''', built by '''{{ill|Ramesses II|de|Ramses II.}}''' Amada relief of Thutmose III.jpg|{{ill|Thutmose III|de|Thutmosis III.}} in the temple of '''Amada''' before '''Re-Harakhte''' - 001 Torino Museo Egizio geroglifici su pietra.jpg|Cartouches of '''Aten''' (First didactic name of Aten) mentioning '''Re-Harakhte''' in the first '''{{ill|cartouche (Ancient Egypt)|de|Kartusche (Altes Ägypten)}}''' (read from right to left) Großer Tempel (Abu Simbel) 18.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''' Flickr - archer10 (Dennis) - Egypt-10C-024.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''', sanctuary with '''Re-Harakhte, Ramesses II, {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re}}, and {{ill|Ptah|de|Ptah}}''' (from right to left) Maler der Grabkammer der Nefertari 001.jpg|'''{{ill|Amentet|de|Amentet}}''' in the form of '''{{ill|Hathor|de|Hathor}}''' with '''Re-Harakhte''' (Tomb of '''{{ill|Nefertari (19th Dynasty)|de|Nefertari (19. Dynastie)}}''', '''{{ill|QV66|de|QV66}}''') == Reception in the New Religious Movement Thelema == In '''{{ill|Thelema|de|Thelema}}''', a '''new religious movement founded by {{ill|Aleister Crowley|de|Aleister Crowley}}''', Re-Harakhte is known as '''"Ra-Hoor-Khuit"''' and represents '''one of the three central principles of Thelemic doctrine.''' "Ra-Hoor-Khuit" appears as the speaker of the '''third chapter''' of '''{{ill|Liber AL vel Legis|de|Liber AL vel Legis}}''', the '''sacred text of Thelema.'''Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian deities|de|Liste ägyptischer Götter}} == References == * '''Manfred Lurker''': ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Special edition, Scherz, Bern, 1998, ISBN 3-502-16430-4, p. 91. * '''Rolf Felde''': ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' 2nd expanded and improved edition, R. Felde Publishing, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * '''Hans Bonnet''': ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Religionsgeschichte.'' Nikol, Hamburg, 2000, ISBN 3-937872-08-6, pp. 269, 629–630. == External links == {{Commonscat|Ra-Horakhty|Re-Harakhte}} == Notes == [[Category:Egyptian deities]] [[Category:Solar deities]] However, in '''the first so-called "{{ill|Aten|lt=Didactic name of Aten|de|Aton#Der lehrhafte Name des Aton}}"''' (Years 4–8 of his reign), he described '''Aten''' as '''Re-Harakhte''': *"Long live the '''Horizon Horus''' (Re-Harakhte), who rejoices in the Light Land (Horizon)."* == Cult and {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=Worship Sites}} == As early as '''2445 BCE''' ({{ill|5th Dynasty of Egypt|de|5. Dynastie}}), the '''fusion form of these two gods''' appeared in the '''{{ill|Sun Temple of Nyuserre|de|Sonnenheiligtum des Niuserre}}''' at '''{{ill|Abusir|de|Abusir}}''' ('''{{ill|Abu Ghurab|de|Abu Ghurab}}'''). Re-Harakhte was also worshiped in temples at: - '''Wadi es-Sebua''' - '''{{ill|Amada (Nubian village)|de|Amada (nubisches Dorf)}}''' - '''{{ill|Temple of Derr|de|Tempel von Derr}}''' - The '''{{ill|Great Temple of Abu Simbel|de|Tempel von Abu Simbel}}''', built by '''{{ill|Ramesses II|de|Ramses II.}}''' Amada relief of Thutmose III.jpg|{{ill|Thutmose III|de|Thutmosis III.}} in the temple of '''Amada''' before '''Re-Harakhte''' - 001 Torino Museo Egizio geroglifici su pietra.jpg|Cartouches of '''Aten''' (First didactic name of Aten) mentioning '''Re-Harakhte''' in the first '''{{ill|cartouche (Ancient Egypt)|de|Kartusche (Altes Ägypten)}}''' (read from right to left) Großer Tempel (Abu Simbel) 18.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''' Flickr - archer10 (Dennis) - Egypt-10C-024.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''', sanctuary with '''Re-Harakhte, Ramesses II, {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re}}, and {{ill|Ptah|de|Ptah}}''' (from right to left) Maler der Grabkammer der Nefertari 001.jpg|'''{{ill|Amentet|de|Amentet}}''' in the form of '''{{ill|Hathor|de|Hathor}}''' with '''Re-Harakhte''' (Tomb of '''{{ill|Nefertari (19th Dynasty)|de|Nefertari (19. Dynastie)}}''', '''{{ill|QV66|de|QV66}}''') == Reception in the New Religious Movement Thelema == In '''{{ill|Thelema|de|Thelema}}''', a '''new religious movement founded by {{ill|Aleister Crowley|de|Aleister Crowley}}''', Re-Harakhte is known as '''"Ra-Hoor-Khuit"''' and represents '''one of the three central principles of Thelemic doctrine.''' "Ra-Hoor-Khuit" appears as the speaker of the '''third chapter''' of '''{{ill|Liber AL vel Legis|de|Liber AL vel Legis}}''', the '''sacred text of Thelema.'''Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian deities|de|Liste ägyptischer Götter}} == References == * '''Manfred Lurker''': ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Special edition, Scherz, Bern, 1998, ISBN 3-502-16430-4, p. 91. * '''Rolf Felde''': ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' 2nd expanded and improved edition, R. Felde Publishing, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * '''Hans Bonnet''': ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Religionsgeschichte.'' Nikol, Hamburg, 2000, ISBN 3-937872-08-6, pp. 269, 629–630. == External links == {{Commonscat|Ra-Horakhty|Re-Harakhte}} == Notes == [[Category:Egyptian deities]] [[Category:Solar deities]] {{Infobox Egyptian Deity |IDEOGRAM = G9 |NAME-EXTENDED = Fully written out |NAME = ra:Z1-G7-Axt:t*y |NAME-EXPLANATION = Ra(u)-heru-achti
''Rˁ(w)-ḥr(.w)-3ḫ.tj''
''{{ill|Ra (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)}}-{{ill|Horus|de|Horus}}, Dweller of the Horizon'' |IMAGE1 = Stele Ra-Horakhty Louvre E5789.jpg |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 180px |IMAGE1-CAPTION = Priest Renpetmaa with {{ill|Unguent cone|de|Salbkegel}} before Re-Harakhte (Stele in the {{ill|Louvre|de|Louvre}}, ca. 900 BCE, {{ill|22nd Dynasty of Egypt|de|22. Dynastie}}) }} '''Re-Harakhte''' (also spelled '''Re-Horakhty''', '''Re-Hor-achti''') is a {{ill|solar deity|de|Sonnengott}} in {{ill|Egyptian mythology|de|Ägyptische Mythologie}}. This god emerged through the fusion ({{ill|Syncretism (Egyptology)|de|Synkretismus (Ägyptologie)}}) of {{ill|Ra (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)}} and {{ill|Horakhty|de|Harachte}}. == Depiction == Re-Harakhte is typically portrayed as a '''standing or seated man''' with '''red skin''' and a '''falcon head'''. On his head, he wears the '''{{ill|solar disk|de|Sonnenscheibe}}''', encircled by the '''{{ill|Uraeus serpent|de|Uräusschlange (Symbol)}}'''. Other representations depict him as a '''human with a ram's head''' and a '''solar disk''', such as in the '''{{ill|Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|lt=Mortuary Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|de|Totentempel des Sethos I. (Abydos)}}''', or as having a '''lion, cat, or falcon head with ram horns and a solar disk''', particularly in '''({{ill|Hibis|de|Hibis}}).''' == Meaning == Re-Harakhte describes Ra as the '''rising sun in the east.''' It is the actual name of the god '''Ra of Heliopolis.'''Rolf Felde: ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' Wiesbaden, 1995, p. 51. As the '''Heliopolitan sun god''', he is highlighted as the '''"ruler of the world."''' In {{ill|Edfu|de|Edfu}}, '''Re-Harakhte of Heliopolis''' was regarded as '''identical to the god {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}}.'''M. Lurker: ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Bern, 1998, p. 91. {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao}} '''{{ill|Akhenaten|de|Echnaton}}''' called himself the '''"High Priest of Re-Harakhte"''', and at the start of his reign, he depicted the god in his traditional form. However, in '''the first so-called "{{ill|Aten|lt=Didactic name of Aten|de|Aton#Der lehrhafte Name des Aton}}"''' (Years 4–8 of his reign), he described '''Aten''' as '''Re-Harakhte''': *"Long live the '''Horizon Horus''' (Re-Harakhte), who rejoices in the Light Land (Horizon)."* == Cult and {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=Worship Sites}} == As early as '''2445 BCE''' ({{ill|5th Dynasty of Egypt|de|5. Dynastie}}), the '''fusion form of these two gods''' appeared in the '''{{ill|Sun Temple of Nyuserre|de|Sonnenheiligtum des Niuserre}}''' at '''{{ill|Abusir|de|Abusir}}''' ('''{{ill|Abu Ghurab|de|Abu Ghurab}}'''). Re-Harakhte was also worshiped in temples at: - '''Wadi es-Sebua''' - '''{{ill|Amada (Nubian village)|de|Amada (nubisches Dorf)}}''' - '''{{ill|Temple of Derr|de|Tempel von Derr}}''' - The '''{{ill|Great Temple of Abu Simbel|de|Tempel von Abu Simbel}}''', built by '''{{ill|Ramesses II|de|Ramses II.}}''' Amada relief of Thutmose III.jpg|{{ill|Thutmose III|de|Thutmosis III.}} in the temple of '''Amada''' before '''Re-Harakhte''' - 001 Torino Museo Egizio geroglifici su pietra.jpg|Cartouches of '''Aten''' (First didactic name of Aten) mentioning '''Re-Harakhte''' in the first '''{{ill|cartouche (Ancient Egypt)|de|Kartusche (Altes Ägypten)}}''' (read from right to left) Großer Tempel (Abu Simbel) 18.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''' Flickr - archer10 (Dennis) - Egypt-10C-024.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''', sanctuary with '''Re-Harakhte, Ramesses II, {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re}}, and {{ill|Ptah|de|Ptah}}''' (from right to left) Maler der Grabkammer der Nefertari 001.jpg|'''{{ill|Amentet|de|Amentet}}''' in the form of '''{{ill|Hathor|de|Hathor}}''' with '''Re-Harakhte''' (Tomb of '''{{ill|Nefertari (19th Dynasty)|de|Nefertari (19. Dynastie)}}''', '''{{ill|QV66|de|QV66}}''') == Reception in the New Religious Movement Thelema == In '''{{ill|Thelema|de|Thelema}}''', a '''new religious movement founded by {{ill|Aleister Crowley|de|Aleister Crowley}}''', Re-Harakhte is known as '''"Ra-Hoor-Khuit"''' and represents '''one of the three central principles of Thelemic doctrine.''' "Ra-Hoor-Khuit" appears as the speaker of the '''third chapter''' of '''{{ill|Liber AL vel Legis|de|Liber AL vel Legis}}''', the '''sacred text of Thelema.'''Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian deities|de|Liste ägyptischer Götter}} == References == * '''Manfred Lurker''': ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Special edition, Scherz, Bern, 1998, ISBN 3-502-16430-4, p. 91. * '''Rolf Felde''': ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' 2nd expanded and improved edition, R. Felde Publishing, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * '''Hans Bonnet''': ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Religionsgeschichte.'' Nikol, Hamburg, 2000, ISBN 3-937872-08-6, pp. 269, 629–630. == External links == {{Commonscat|Ra-Horakhty|Re-Harakhte}} == Notes == [[Category:Egyptian deities]] [[Category:Solar deities]] However, in '''the first so-called "{{ill|Aten|lt=Didactic name of Aten|de|Aton#Der lehrhafte Name des Aton}}"''' (Years 4–8 of his reign), he described '''Aten''' as '''Re-Harakhte''': *"Long live the '''Horizon Horus''' (Re-Harakhte), who rejoices in the Light Land (Horizon)."* == Cult and {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=Worship Sites}} == As early as '''2445 BCE''' ({{ill|5th Dynasty of Egypt|de|5. Dynastie}}), the '''fusion form of these two gods''' appeared in the '''{{ill|Sun Temple of Nyuserre|de|Sonnenheiligtum des Niuserre}}''' at '''{{ill|Abusir|de|Abusir}}''' ('''{{ill|Abu Ghurab|de|Abu Ghurab}}'''). Re-Harakhte was also worshiped in temples at: * '''Wadi es-Sebua''' * '''{{ill|Amada (Nubian village)|de|Amada (nubisches Dorf)}}''' * '''{{ill|Temple of Derr|de|Tempel von Derr}}''' - The '''{{ill|Great Temple of Abu Simbel|de|Tempel von Abu Simbel}}''', built by '''{{ill|Ramesses II|de|Ramses II.}}''' Amada relief of Thutmose III.jpg|{{ill|Thutmose III|de|Thutmosis III.}} in the temple of '''Amada''' before '''Re-Harakhte''' - 001 Torino Museo Egizio geroglifici su pietra.jpg|Cartouches of '''Aten''' (First didactic name of Aten) mentioning '''Re-Harakhte''' in the first '''{{ill|cartouche (Ancient Egypt)|de|Kartusche (Altes Ägypten)}}''' (read from right to left) Großer Tempel (Abu Simbel) 18.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''' Flickr - archer10 (Dennis) - Egypt-10C-024.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''', sanctuary with '''Re-Harakhte, Ramesses II, {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re}}, and {{ill|Ptah|de|Ptah}}''' (from right to left) Maler der Grabkammer der Nefertari 001.jpg|'''{{ill|Amentet|de|Amentet}}''' in the form of '''{{ill|Hathor|de|Hathor}}''' with '''Re-Harakhte''' (Tomb of '''{{ill|Nefertari (19th Dynasty)|de|Nefertari (19. Dynastie)}}''', '''{{ill|QV66|de|QV66}}''') == Reception in the New Religious Movement Thelema == In '''{{ill|Thelema|de|Thelema}}''', a '''new religious movement founded by {{ill|Aleister Crowley|de|Aleister Crowley}}''', Re-Harakhte is known as '''"Ra-Hoor-Khuit"''' and represents '''one of the three central principles of Thelemic doctrine.''' "Ra-Hoor-Khuit" appears as the speaker of the '''third chapter''' of '''{{ill|Liber AL vel Legis|de|Liber AL vel Legis}}''', the '''sacred text of Thelema.'''Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian deities|de|Liste ägyptischer Götter}} == References == * '''Manfred Lurker''': ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Special edition, Scherz, Bern, 1998, ISBN 3-502-16430-4, p. 91. * '''Rolf Felde''': ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' 2nd expanded and improved edition, R. Felde Publishing, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * '''Hans Bonnet''': ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Religionsgeschichte.'' Nikol, Hamburg, 2000, ISBN 3-937872-08-6, pp. 269, 629–630. == External links == {{Commonscat|Ra-Horakhty|Re-Harakhte}} == Notes == [[Category:Egyptian deities]] [[Category:Solar deities]] {{Infobox Egyptian Deity |IDEOGRAM = G9 |NAME-EXTENDED = Fully written out |NAME = ra:Z1-G7-Axt:t*y |NAME-EXPLANATION = Ra(u)-heru-achti
''Rˁ(w)-ḥr(.w)-3ḫ.tj''
''{{ill|Ra (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)}}-{{ill|Horus|de|Horus}}, Dweller of the Horizon'' |IMAGE1 = Stele Ra-Horakhty Louvre E5789.jpg |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 180px |IMAGE1-CAPTION = Priest Renpetmaa with {{ill|Unguent cone|de|Salbkegel}} before Re-Harakhte (Stele in the {{ill|Louvre|de|Louvre}}, ca. 900 BCE, {{ill|22nd Dynasty of Egypt|de|22. Dynastie}}) }} '''Re-Harakhte''' (also spelled '''Re-Horakhty''', '''Re-Hor-achti''') is a {{ill|solar deity|de|Sonnengott}} in {{ill|Egyptian mythology|de|Ägyptische Mythologie}}. This god emerged through the fusion ({{ill|Syncretism (Egyptology)|de|Synkretismus (Ägyptologie)}}) of {{ill|Ra (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)}} and {{ill|Horakhty|de|Harachte}}. == Depiction == Re-Harakhte is typically portrayed as a '''standing or seated man''' with '''red skin''' and a '''falcon head'''. On his head, he wears the '''{{ill|solar disk|de|Sonnenscheibe}}''', encircled by the '''{{ill|Uraeus serpent|de|Uräusschlange (Symbol)}}'''. Other representations depict him as a '''human with a ram's head''' and a '''solar disk''', such as in the '''{{ill|Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|lt=Mortuary Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|de|Totentempel des Sethos I. (Abydos)}}''', or as having a '''lion, cat, or falcon head with ram horns and a solar disk''', particularly in '''({{ill|Hibis|de|Hibis}}).''' == Meaning == Re-Harakhte describes Ra as the '''rising sun in the east.''' It is the actual name of the god '''Ra of Heliopolis.'''Rolf Felde: ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' Wiesbaden, 1995, p. 51. As the '''Heliopolitan sun god''', he is highlighted as the '''"ruler of the world."''' In {{ill|Edfu|de|Edfu}}, '''Re-Harakhte of Heliopolis''' was regarded as '''identical to the god {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}}.'''M. Lurker: ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Bern, 1998, p. 91. {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao}} '''{{ill|Akhenaten|de|Echnaton}}''' called himself the '''"High Priest of Re-Harakhte"''', and at the start of his reign, he depicted the god in his traditional form. However, in '''the first so-called "{{ill|Aten|lt=Didactic name of Aten|de|Aton#Der lehrhafte Name des Aton}}"''' (Years 4–8 of his reign), he described '''Aten''' as '''Re-Harakhte''': *"Long live the '''Horizon Horus''' (Re-Harakhte), who rejoices in the Light Land (Horizon)."* == Cult and {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=Worship Sites}} == As early as '''2445 BCE''' ({{ill|5th Dynasty of Egypt|de|5. Dynastie}}), the '''fusion form of these two gods''' appeared in the '''{{ill|Sun Temple of Nyuserre|de|Sonnenheiligtum des Niuserre}}''' at '''{{ill|Abusir|de|Abusir}}''' ('''{{ill|Abu Ghurab|de|Abu Ghurab}}'''). Re-Harakhte was also worshiped in temples at: * '''Wadi es-Sebua''' * '''{{ill|Amada (Nubian village)|de|Amada (nubisches Dorf)}}''' * '''{{ill|Temple of Derr|de|Tempel von Derr}}''' - The '''{{ill|Great Temple of Abu Simbel|de|Tempel von Abu Simbel}}''', built by '''{{ill|Ramesses II|de|Ramses II.}}''' Amada relief of Thutmose III.jpg|{{ill|Thutmose III|de|Thutmosis III.}} in the temple of '''Amada''' before '''Re-Harakhte''' - 001 Torino Museo Egizio geroglifici su pietra.jpg|Cartouches of '''Aten''' (First didactic name of Aten) mentioning '''Re-Harakhte''' in the first '''{{ill|cartouche (Ancient Egypt)|de|Kartusche (Altes Ägypten)}}''' (read from right to left) Großer Tempel (Abu Simbel) 18.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''' Flickr - archer10 (Dennis) - Egypt-10C-024.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''', sanctuary with '''Re-Harakhte, Ramesses II, {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re}}, and {{ill|Ptah|de|Ptah}}''' (from right to left) Maler der Grabkammer der Nefertari 001.jpg|'''{{ill|Amentet|de|Amentet}}''' in the form of '''{{ill|Hathor|de|Hathor}}''' with '''Re-Harakhte''' (Tomb of '''{{ill|Nefertari (19th Dynasty)|de|Nefertari (19. Dynastie)}}''', '''{{ill|QV66|de|QV66}}''') == Reception in the New Religious Movement Thelema == In '''{{ill|Thelema|de|Thelema}}''', a '''new religious movement founded by {{ill|Aleister Crowley|de|Aleister Crowley}}''', Re-Harakhte is known as '''"Ra-Hoor-Khuit"''' and represents '''one of the three central principles of Thelemic doctrine.''' "Ra-Hoor-Khuit" appears as the speaker of the '''third chapter''' of '''{{ill|Liber AL vel Legis|de|Liber AL vel Legis}}''', the '''sacred text of Thelema.'''Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian deities|de|Liste ägyptischer Götter}} == References == * '''Manfred Lurker''': ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Special edition, Scherz, Bern, 1998, ISBN 3-502-16430-4, p. 91. * '''Rolf Felde''': ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' 2nd expanded and improved edition, R. Felde Publishing, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * '''Hans Bonnet''': ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Religionsgeschichte.'' Bern, 1998, p. 91. {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao}} '''{{ill|Akhenaten|de|Echnaton}}''' called himself the '''"High Priest of Re-Harakhte"''', and at the start of his reign, he depicted the god in his traditional form. However, in '''the first so-called "{{ill|Aten|lt=Didactic name of Aten|de|Aton#Der lehrhafte Name des Aton}}"''' (Years 4–8 of his reign), he described '''Aten''' as '''Re-Harakhte''': *"Long live the '''Horizon Horus''' (Re-Harakhte), who rejoices in the Light Land (Horizon)."* == Cult and {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=Worship Sites}} == As early as '''2445 BCE''' ({{ill|5th Dynasty of Egypt|de|5. Dynastie}}), the '''fusion form of these two gods''' appeared in the '''{{ill|Sun Temple of Nyuserre|de|Sonnenheiligtum des Niuserre}}''' at '''{{ill|Abusir|de|Abusir}}''' ('''{{ill|Abu Ghurab|de|Abu Ghurab}}'''). Re-Harakhte was also worshiped in temples at: * '''Wadi es-Sebua''' * '''{{ill|Amada (Nubian village)|de|Amada (nubisches Dorf)}}''' * '''{{ill|Temple of Derr|de|Tempel von Derr}}''' - The '''{{ill|Great Temple of Abu Simbel|de|Tempel von Abu Simbel}}''', built by '''{{ill|Ramesses II|de|Ramses II.}}''' Amada relief of Thutmose III.jpg|{{ill|Thutmose III|de|Thutmosis III.}} in the temple of '''Amada''' before '''Re-Harakhte''' - 001 Torino Museo Egizio geroglifici su pietra.jpg|Cartouches of '''Aten''' (First didactic name of Aten) mentioning '''Re-Harakhte''' in the first '''{{ill|cartouche (Ancient Egypt)|de|Kartusche (Altes Ägypten)}}''' (read from right to left) Großer Tempel (Abu Simbel) 18.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''' Flickr - archer10 (Dennis) - Egypt-10C-024.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''', sanctuary with '''Re-Harakhte, Ramesses II, {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re}}, and {{ill|Ptah|de|Ptah}}''' (from right to left) Maler der Grabkammer der Nefertari 001.jpg|'''{{ill|Amentet|de|Amentet}}''' in the form of '''{{ill|Hathor|de|Hathor}}''' with '''Re-Harakhte''' (Tomb of '''{{ill|Nefertari (19th Dynasty)|de|Nefertari (19. Dynastie)}}''', '''{{ill|QV66|de|QV66}}''') == Reception in the New Religious Movement Thelema == In '''{{ill|Thelema|de|Thelema}}''', a '''new religious movement founded by {{ill|Aleister Crowley|de|Aleister Crowley}}''', Re-Harakhte is known as '''"Ra-Hoor-Khuit"''' and represents '''one of the three central principles of Thelemic doctrine.''' "Ra-Hoor-Khuit" appears as the speaker of the '''third chapter''' of '''{{ill|Liber AL vel Legis|de|Liber AL vel Legis}}''', the '''sacred text of Thelema.'''Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian deities|de|Liste ägyptischer Götter}} == References == * '''Manfred Lurker''': ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Special edition, Scherz, Bern, 1998, ISBN 3-502-16430-4, p. 91. * '''Rolf Felde''': ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' 2nd expanded and improved edition, R. Felde Publishing, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * '''Hans Bonnet''': ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Religionsgeschichte.'' {{Infobox Egyptian Deity |IDEOGRAM = G9 |NAME-EXTENDED = Fully written out |NAME = ra:Z1-G7-Axt:t*y |NAME-EXPLANATION = Ra(u)-heru-achti
''Rˁ(w)-ḥr(.w)-3ḫ.tj''
''{{ill|Ra (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)}}-{{ill|Horus|de|Horus}}, Dweller of the Horizon'' |IMAGE1 = Stele Ra-Horakhty Louvre E5789.jpg |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 180px |IMAGE1-CAPTION = Priest Renpetmaa with {{ill|Unguent cone|de|Salbkegel}} before Re-Harakhte (Stele in the {{ill|Louvre|de|Louvre}}, ca. 900 BCE, {{ill|22nd Dynasty of Egypt|de|22. Dynastie}}) }} '''Re-Harakhte''' (also spelled '''Re-Horakhty''', '''Re-Hor-achti''') is a {{ill|solar deity|de|Sonnengott}} in {{ill|Egyptian mythology|de|Ägyptische Mythologie}}. This god emerged through the fusion ({{ill|Syncretism (Egyptology)|de|Synkretismus (Ägyptologie)}}) of {{ill|Ra (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)}} and {{ill|Horakhty|de|Harachte}}. == Depiction == Re-Harakhte is typically portrayed as a '''standing or seated man''' with '''red skin''' and a '''falcon head'''. On his head, he wears the '''{{ill|solar disk|de|Sonnenscheibe}}''', encircled by the '''{{ill|Uraeus|lt=Uraeus serpent|de|Uräusschlange (Symbol)}}'''. Other representations depict him as a '''human with a ram's head''' and a '''solar disk''', such as in the '''{{ill|Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|lt=Mortuary Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|de|Totentempel des Sethos I. (Abydos)}}''', or as having a '''lion, cat, or falcon head with ram horns and a solar disk''', particularly in '''({{ill|Hibis|de|Hibis}}).''' == Meaning == Re-Harakhte describes Ra as the '''rising sun in the east.''' It is the actual name of the god '''Ra of Heliopolis.'''Rolf Felde: ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' Wiesbaden, 1995, p. 51. As the '''Heliopolitan sun god''', he is highlighted as the '''"ruler of the world."''' In {{ill|Edfu|de|Edfu}}, '''Re-Harakhte of Heliopolis''' was regarded as '''identical to the god {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}}.'''M. Lurker: ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Bern, 1998, p. 91. {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao}} '''{{ill|Akhenaten|de|Echnaton}}''' called himself the '''"High Priest of Re-Harakhte"''', and at the start of his reign, he depicted the god in his traditional form. However, in '''the first so-called "{{ill|Aten|lt=Didactic name of Aten|de|Aton#Der lehrhafte Name des Aton}}"''' (Years 4–8 of his reign), he described '''Aten''' as '''Re-Harakhte''': *"Long live the '''Horizon Horus''' (Re-Harakhte), who rejoices in the Light Land (Horizon)."* == Cult and {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=Worship Sites}} == As early as '''2445 BCE''' ({{ill|5th Dynasty of Egypt|de|5. Dynastie}}), the '''fusion form of these two gods''' appeared in the '''{{ill|Sun Temple of Nyuserre|de|Sonnenheiligtum des Niuserre}}''' at '''{{ill|Abusir|de|Abusir}}''' ('''{{ill|Abu Ghurab|de|Abu Ghurab}}'''). Re-Harakhte was also worshiped in temples at: * '''Wadi es-Sebua''' * '''{{ill|Amada (Nubian village)|de|Amada (nubisches Dorf)}}''' * '''{{ill|Temple of Derr|de|Tempel von Derr}}''' - The '''{{ill|Great Temple of Abu Simbel|de|Tempel von Abu Simbel}}''', built by '''{{ill|Ramesses II|de|Ramses II.}}''' Amada relief of Thutmose III.jpg|{{ill|Thutmose III|de|Thutmosis III.}} in the temple of '''Amada''' before '''Re-Harakhte''' - 001 Torino Museo Egizio geroglifici su pietra.jpg|Cartouches of '''Aten''' (First didactic name of Aten) mentioning '''Re-Harakhte''' in the first '''{{ill|cartouche (Ancient Egypt)|de|Kartusche (Altes Ägypten)}}''' (read from right to left) Großer Tempel (Abu Simbel) 18.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''' Flickr - archer10 (Dennis) - Egypt-10C-024.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''', sanctuary with '''Re-Harakhte, Ramesses II, {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re}}, and {{ill|Ptah|de|Ptah}}''' (from right to left) Maler der Grabkammer der Nefertari 001.jpg|'''{{ill|Amentet|de|Amentet}}''' in the form of '''{{ill|Hathor|de|Hathor}}''' with '''Re-Harakhte''' (Tomb of '''{{ill|Nefertari (19th Dynasty)|de|Nefertari (19. Dynastie)}}''', '''{{ill|QV66|de|QV66}}''') == Reception in the New Religious Movement Thelema == In '''{{ill|Thelema|de|Thelema}}''', a '''new religious movement founded by {{ill|Aleister Crowley|de|Aleister Crowley}}''', Re-Harakhte is known as '''"Ra-Hoor-Khuit"''' and represents '''one of the three central principles of Thelemic doctrine.''' "Ra-Hoor-Khuit" appears as the speaker of the '''third chapter''' of '''{{ill|Liber AL vel Legis|de|Liber AL vel Legis}}''', the '''sacred text of Thelema.'''Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian deities|de|Liste ägyptischer Götter}} == References == * '''Manfred Lurker''': ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Special edition, Scherz, Bern, 1998, ISBN 3-502-16430-4, p. 91. * '''Rolf Felde''': ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' 2nd expanded and improved edition, R. Felde Publishing, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * '''Hans Bonnet''': ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Religionsgeschichte.'' In {{ill|Edfu|de|Edfu}}, '''Re-Harakhte of Heliopolis''' was regarded as '''identical to the god {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}}.'''M. Lurker: ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Bern, 1998, p. 91. {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao}} '''{{ill|Akhenaten|de|Echnaton}}''' called himself the '''"High Priest of Re-Harakhte"''', and at the start of his reign, he depicted the god in his traditional form. However, in '''the first so-called "{{ill|Aten|lt=Didactic name of Aten|de|Aton#Der lehrhafte Name des Aton}}"''' (Years 4–8 of his reign), he described '''Aten''' as '''Re-Harakhte''': *"Long live the '''Horizon Horus''' (Re-Harakhte), who rejoices in the Light Land (Horizon)."* == Cult and {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=Worship Sites}} == As early as '''2445 BCE''' ({{ill|5th Dynasty of Egypt|de|5. Dynastie}}), the '''fusion form of these two gods''' appeared in the '''{{ill|Sun Temple of Nyuserre|de|Sonnenheiligtum des Niuserre}}''' at '''{{ill|Abusir|de|Abusir}}''' ('''{{ill|Abu Ghurab|de|Abu Ghurab}}'''). Re-Harakhte was also worshiped in temples at: * '''Wadi es-Sebua''' * '''{{ill|Amada (Nubian village)|de|Amada (nubisches Dorf)}}''' * '''{{ill|Temple of Derr|de|Tempel von Derr}}''' - The '''{{ill|Great Temple of Abu Simbel|de|Tempel von Abu Simbel}}''', built by '''{{ill|Ramesses II|de|Ramses II.}}''' Amada relief of Thutmose III.jpg|{{ill|Thutmose III|de|Thutmosis III.}} in the temple of '''Amada''' before '''Re-Harakhte''' - 001 Torino Museo Egizio geroglifici su pietra.jpg|Cartouches of '''Aten''' (First didactic name of Aten) mentioning '''Re-Harakhte''' in the first '''{{ill|cartouche (Ancient Egypt)|de|Kartusche (Altes Ägypten)}}''' (read from right to left) Großer Tempel (Abu Simbel) 18.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''' Flickr - archer10 (Dennis) - Egypt-10C-024.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''', sanctuary with '''Re-Harakhte, Ramesses II, {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re}}, and {{ill|Ptah|de|Ptah}}''' (from right to left) Maler der Grabkammer der Nefertari 001.jpg|'''{{ill|Amentet|de|Amentet}}''' in the form of '''{{ill|Hathor|de|Hathor}}''' with '''Re-Harakhte''' (Tomb of '''{{ill|Nefertari (19th Dynasty)|de|Nefertari (19. Dynastie)}}''', '''{{ill|QV66|de|QV66}}''') == Reception in the New Religious Movement Thelema == In '''{{ill|Thelema|de|Thelema}}''', a '''new religious movement founded by {{ill|Aleister Crowley|de|Aleister Crowley}}''', Re-Harakhte is known as '''"Ra-Hoor-Khuit"''' and represents '''one of the three central principles of Thelemic doctrine.''' "Ra-Hoor-Khuit" appears as the speaker of the '''third chapter''' of '''{{ill|Liber AL vel Legis|de|Liber AL vel Legis}}''', the '''sacred text of Thelema.'''Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian deities|de|Liste ägyptischer Götter}} == References == * '''Manfred Lurker''': ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Special edition, Scherz, Bern, 1998, ISBN 3-502-16430-4, p. 91. * '''Rolf Felde''': ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' 2nd expanded and improved edition, R. Felde Publishing, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * '''Hans Bonnet''': ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Religionsgeschichte.'' Nikol, Hamburg, 2000, ISBN 3-937872-08-6, pp. 269, 629–630. == External links == {{Commonscat|Ra-Horakhty|Re-Harakhte}} == Notes == [[Category:Egyptian deities]] [[Category:Solar deities]] {{Infobox Egyptian Deity |IDEOGRAM = G9 |NAME-EXTENDED = Fully written out |NAME = ra:Z1-G7-Axt:t*y |NAME-EXPLANATION = Ra(u)-heru-achti
''Rˁ(w)-ḥr(.w)-3ḫ.tj''
''{{ill|Ra (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)|1=Ra}}-{{ill|Horus|de|Horus}}, Dweller of the Horizon'' |IMAGE1 = Stele Ra-Horakhty Louvre E5789.jpg |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 180px |IMAGE1-CAPTION = Priest Renpetmaa with {{ill|Unguent cone|de|Salbkegel}} before Re-Harakhte (Stele in the {{ill|Louvre|de|Louvre}}, ca. 900 BCE, {{ill|22nd Dynasty of Egypt|de|22. Dynastie}}) }} '''Re-Harakhte''' (also spelled '''Re-Horakhty''', '''Re-Hor-achti''') is a {{ill|solar deity|de|Sonnengott}} in {{ill|Egyptian mythology|de|Ägyptische Mythologie}}. This god emerged through the fusion ({{ill|Syncretism (Egyptology)|de|Synkretismus (Ägyptologie)}}) of {{ill|Ra (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)|1=Ra}} and {{ill|Horakhty|de|Harachte}}. == Depiction == Re-Harakhte is typically portrayed as a '''standing or seated man''' with '''red skin''' and a '''falcon head'''. On his head, he wears the '''{{ill|solar disk|de|Sonnenscheibe}}''', encircled by the '''{{ill|Uraeus|lt=Uraeus serpent|de|Uräusschlange (Symbol)}}'''. Other representations depict him as a '''human with a ram's head''' and a '''solar disk''', such as in the '''{{ill|Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|lt=Mortuary Temple of Seti I (Abydos)|de|Totentempel des Sethos I. (Abydos)}}''', or as having a '''lion, cat, or falcon head with ram horns and a solar disk''', particularly in '''({{ill|Hibis|de|Hibis}}).''' == Meaning == Re-Harakhte describes Ra as the '''rising sun in the east.''' It is the actual name of the god '''Ra of Heliopolis.'''Rolf Felde: ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' Wiesbaden, 1995, p. 51. As the '''Heliopolitan sun god''', he is highlighted as the '''"ruler of the world."''' In {{ill|Edfu|de|Edfu}}, '''Re-Harakhte of Heliopolis''' was regarded as '''identical to the god {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}}.'''M. Lurker: ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Bern, 1998, p. 91. {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao}} '''{{ill|Akhenaten|de|Echnaton}}''' called himself the '''"High Priest of Re-Harakhte"''', and at the start of his reign, he depicted the god in his traditional form. However, in '''the first so-called "{{ill|Aten|lt=Didactic name of Aten|de|Aton#Der lehrhafte Name des Aton}}"''' (Years 4–8 of his reign), he described '''Aten''' as '''Re-Harakhte''': *"Long live the '''Horizon Horus''' (Re-Harakhte), who rejoices in the Light Land (Horizon)."* == Cult and {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=Worship Sites}} == As early as '''2445 BCE''' ({{ill|5th Dynasty of Egypt|de|5. Dynastie}}), the '''fusion form of these two gods''' appeared in the '''{{ill|Sun Temple of Nyuserre|de|Sonnenheiligtum des Niuserre}}''' at '''{{ill|Abusir|de|Abusir}}''' ('''{{ill|Abu Ghurab|de|Abu Ghurab}}'''). Re-Harakhte was also worshiped in temples at: * '''Wadi es-Sebua''' * '''{{ill|Amada (Nubian village)|de|Amada (nubisches Dorf)}}''' * '''{{ill|Temple of Derr|de|Tempel von Derr}}''' - The '''{{ill|Great Temple of Abu Simbel|de|Tempel von Abu Simbel}}''', built by '''{{ill|Ramesses II|de|Ramses II.}}''' Amada relief of Thutmose III.jpg|{{ill|Thutmose III|de|Thutmosis III.}} in the temple of '''Amada''' before '''Re-Harakhte''' - 001 Torino Museo Egizio geroglifici su pietra.jpg|Cartouches of '''Aten''' (First didactic name of Aten) mentioning '''Re-Harakhte''' in the first '''{{ill|cartouche (Ancient Egypt)|de|Kartusche (Altes Ägypten)}}''' (read from right to left) Großer Tempel (Abu Simbel) 18.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''' Flickr - archer10 (Dennis) - Egypt-10C-024.jpg|'''Great Temple of Abu Simbel''', sanctuary with '''Re-Harakhte, Ramesses II, {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re}}, and {{ill|Ptah|de|Ptah}}''' (from right to left) Maler der Grabkammer der Nefertari 001.jpg|'''{{ill|Amentet|de|Amentet}}''' in the form of '''{{ill|Hathor|de|Hathor}}''' with '''Re-Harakhte''' (Tomb of '''{{ill|Nefertari (19th Dynasty)|de|Nefertari (19. Dynastie)}}''', '''{{ill|QV66|de|QV66}}''') == Reception in the New Religious Movement Thelema == In '''{{ill|Thelema|de|Thelema}}''', a '''new religious movement founded by {{ill|Aleister Crowley|de|Aleister Crowley}}''', Re-Harakhte is known as '''"Ra-Hoor-Khuit"''' and represents '''one of the three central principles of Thelemic doctrine.''' "Ra-Hoor-Khuit" appears as the speaker of the '''third chapter''' of '''{{ill|Liber AL vel Legis|de|Liber AL vel Legis}}''', the '''sacred text of Thelema.'''Rodney Orpheus: ''Abrahadabra: Understanding Aleister Crowley's Thelemic Magick.'' Weiser, Boston MA, 2005, ISBN 1-57863-326-5, pp. 33–44. == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian deities|de|Liste ägyptischer Götter}} == References == * '''Manfred Lurker''': ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Special edition, Scherz, Bern, 1998, ISBN 3-502-16430-4, p. 91. * '''Rolf Felde''': ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' 2nd expanded and improved edition, R. Felde Publishing, Wiesbaden, 1995, pp. 50–51. * '''Hans Bonnet''': ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Religionsgeschichte.'' Nikol, Hamburg, 2000, ISBN 3-937872-08-6, pp. 269, 629–630. == External links == {{Commonscat|Ra-Horakhty|Re-Harakhte}} == Notes == [[Category:Egyptian deities]] [[Category:Solar deities]] As the '''Heliopolitan sun god''', he is highlighted as the '''"ruler of the world."''' In {{ill|Edfu|de|Edfu}}, '''Re-Harakhte of Heliopolis''' was regarded as '''identical to the god {{ill|Horus|de|Horus}}.'''M. Lurker: ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Dynastie}}), the '''fusion form of these two gods''' appeared in the '''{{ill|Sun Temple of Nyuserre|de|Sonnenheiligtum des Niuserre}}''' at '''{{ill|Abusir|de|Abusir}}''' ('''{{ill|Abu Ghurab|de|Abu Ghurab}}''').
